H &= 14 \, Q_h^{0.3} \\
H &= 14 \times (80)^{0.3} \\
&= 14 \times e^{0.3 \ln 80} \\
&= 14 \times e^{0.3 \times 4.382} \\
&= 14 \times e^{1.3146} \\
&= 14 \times 3.722 \\
&= 52.11 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Required stack height = 52.11 m ≈ 52 m.**

**Comparison:** Actual height (40 m) < Required (52 m). The plant is **non-compliant**.

**Alternative Control Strategies:**
1. **Increase stack height** to ≥ 52 m.
2. **Install Flue Gas Desulphurization (FGD)** — wet limestone-based scrubber can remove ≥ 90% SO₂.
3. **Switch to low-sulphur coal** (< 0.5% S) or natural gas.
4. **Use Circulating Fluidized Bed Combustion (CFBC)** with in-bed limestone sorbent injection.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Set B]`

**(a)** Define ambient air quality. With a neat diagram, explain the working of a Hi-Volume Sampler used for ambient PM₁₀ monitoring. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Ambient air quality is the measure of pollutant concentrations in the outdoor atmospheric air to which the general public has exposed access, assessed against NAAQS limits.

**Hi-Volume Sampler (HVS) Principle:**
- Operates at a high flow rate of **1.0–1.5 m³/min** (hence "hi-volume").
- Draws ambient air through a **filter paper** (glass fibre / quartz) housed in a protective shelter placed 2–10 m above ground.

**Working:**
1. A blower/motor pulls ambient air through a size-selective inlet.
2. PM₁₀ inlet allows only particles < 10 µm aerodynamic diameter to enter.
3. Particles deposit on the pre-weighed filter paper.
4. Sampling runs for **24 hours**.
5. Filter is reweighed post-sampling.
6. PM₁₀ concentration = (Final mass − Initial mass) / Volume of air sampled.

$$C = \frac{(W_f - W_i) \times 10^6}{V} \quad \text{µg/m}^3$$

where $V$ = total volume of air sampled in m³, $W$ in grams.

**(b)** Explain the conversion between ppm and µg/m³ with a derivation. Convert 50 ppb of NO₂ to µg/m³ at 25°C, 1 atm. (MW of NO₂ = 46 g/mol) **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The conversion uses the molar volume at standard conditions:

$$C_{µg/m^3} = \frac{C_{ppm} \times MW}{24.45}$$

For **ppb** (parts per billion), since 1 ppm = 1000 ppb:

$$C_{µg/m^3} = \frac{C_{ppb} \times MW}{24.45 \times 1000}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{µg/m^3} &= \frac{50 \times 46}{24.45 \times 1000} \\
&= \frac{2300}{24450} \\
&= 0.0941 \text{ µg/m}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** 50 ppb NO₂ = **0.094 µg/m³**, well within NAAQS limits (NO₂ annual = 80 µg/m³).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing averaging periods** — Students often quote the 24-hr NAAQS as if it were annual, losing 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting stack height exponent** — Some write $H = 14 \cdot Q_h$ instead of $14 \cdot Q_h^{0.3}$ (the 0.3 is critical).
> 3. **Using wrong molar volume** — Use **24.45 L/mol at 25 °C**, NOT 22.4 L/mol (which is at 0 °C).
> 4. **AQI trap** — Students report *average* sub-index; CPCB specifies the **maximum** sub-index.
> 5. **Unit confusion** — µg/m³ ≠ ppm. Always state both clearly in solutions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Ambient Air Quality Standards** apply to the *environment*; **Emission Standards** apply to the *source*.
- **NAAQS** (CPCB, 2009) monitors **12 pollutants** with **specific averaging periods** (1-hr / 8-hr / 24-hr / annual).
- **National AQI** uses **8 pollutants** and **linear interpolation** between breakpoints; final AQI = **maximum sub-index**.
- **AQI categories:** Good → Satisfactory → Moderate → Poor → Very Poor → Severe (colour-coded green → maroon).
- **CPCB stack height formula:** $H = 14 \, Q_h^{0.3}$ where $Q_h$ = SO₂ emission in kg/hr.
- **ppm ↔ µg/m³ conversion:** $C_{µg/m^3} = (C_{ppm} \times MW) / 24.45$.
- **ppb ↔ µg/m³ conversion:** $C_{µg/m^3} = (C_{ppb} \times MW) / (24.45 \times 1000)$.
- **Molar volume at 25 °C, 1 atm = 24.45 L/mol** (not 22.4).
- **Emission standards** are sector-specific (cement, power, steel, vehicles, etc.); notify CPCB/MoEFCC schedules for numerical limits.
- **Bharat Stage VI** is the current operative vehicular emission norm in India.
- **NCAP** (National Clean Air Programme, 2019) targets 40% PM reduction in 131 non-attainment cities by 2025–26.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ambient and Stack Sampling and Analysis of Particulate and Gaseous Pollutants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBT726 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ambient and Stack Sampling and Analysis of Particulate and Gaseous Pollutants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Air Quality Monitoring?

**Air Quality Monitoring (AQM)** is the systematic, continuous, or periodic measurement of pollutant concentrations in the atmosphere to evaluate the status of ambient air and the effectiveness of control strategies. Under the KTU 2024 syllabus for OEBT726, AQM is divided into two major engineering domains:

1. **Ambient Air Sampling** – measurement of pollutants in the general outdoor atmosphere breathed by the public.
2. **Stack Sampling (Source Sampling)** – measurement of pollutants at the point of emission from industrial stacks or ducts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Syllabus Anchor:** The ability to design, execute, and interpret both ambient and stack sampling campaigns is a **mandatory Course Outcome (CO1)** – "Estimate the types, sources, and effects of air pollutants and demonstrate proficiency in measurement and analysis techniques."

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: "The Doctor and the Patient"

Imagine a doctor diagnosing a patient:
- **Ambient Air Sampling** is like the **doctor's general check-up** – measuring what the public is exposed to in the environment (blood test, lung capacity). It tells you the *symptoms in the environment*.
- **Stack Sampling** is like **investigating the patient's gut** – going directly to the *source* (a factory chimney) to see how much pollutant is being released per unit time. It tells you the *cause* and the *quantity of disease*.

Without both, you cannot prescribe the right environmental cure (pollution control equipment).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Conceptual Anchor:** Ambient sampling is governed by **NAQI (National Ambient Air Quality Monitoring) standards** (CPCB, India), while stack sampling is governed by **Emission Regulations** and **EPA Methodologies** (e.g., USEPA Method 5 for particulates, Method 6 for SO₂).

---

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

- **PM₂.₅ / PM₁₀** – Particulate Matter ≤ 2.5 μm and ≤ 10 μm aerodynamic diameter.
- **µg/m³** – Micrograms per cubic meter (standard unit for ambient concentration).
- **mg/Nm³** – Milligrams per Normal cubic meter (standard unit for stack gas at 0 °C, 1 atm, dry basis).
- **Isokinetic Sampling** – Sampling at a velocity **equal** to the local gas velocity in the stack.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Ambient Air Sampling – Subsystems

### 2.1.1 Suspended Particulate Matter (SPM) Sampling
The two principal devices used in Indian NAAQS networks:

**(a) High Volume Sampler (HVS / HVAS)**
- Operates at **1.1 to 1.7 m³/min** flow rate.
- Uses **8" × 10" glass fiber filter paper**.
- Measures **Total Suspended Particulates (TSP)**.
- Sampling duration: **24 hours** (8-hour averages are non-standard for HVS).

**(b) Respirable Dust Sampler (RDS)**
- Operates at **~1.1 m³/min** (actual ≈ 1.0–1.2 m³/min).
- Uses a **cyclonic/impaction pre-separator** to remove particles **> 10 μm**.
- Measures **PM₁₀** and **PM₂.₅** (PM₂.₅ requires a separate impactor stage).
- Filter: 20 cm × 25 cm glass fiber or quartz filter.

### 2.1.2 Gaseous Pollutant Sampling
- **Absorption in Liquid** – e.g., SO₂ in **Sodium Tetrachloromercurate (TCM)** solution → West-Gaeke method.
- **Adsorption on Solid** – e.g., VOCs on **Activated Charcoal / Tenax / Carbotrap** tubes.
- **Direct Instrumental Analysis** – NDIR, Chemiluminescence, UV-Fluorescence, FID, PID.
- **Passive (Diffusive) Samplers** – no pump; relies on Fick's first law of diffusion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why pre-separation in RDS?** Because health effects are dominated by **fine particulates** that penetrate deep into alveoli. Larger particles are filtered naturally by the nasal passage and are not the focus of respiratory epidemiology.

---

## 2.2 Stack Sampling – The Isokinetic Principle

### 2.2.1 The Central Problem
A gas stream in a stack carries particles with a certain **inertia and velocity**. If the sampling probe inlet velocity **≠** the local gas velocity, the sample will be **non-representative**:
- If probe velocity **> stack velocity** → **over-sampling of fine particles** (lighter particles follow gas streamlines and enter; heavier particles cannot turn into the probe).
- If probe velocity **< stack velocity** → **over-sampling of coarse particles** (inertia carries heavy particles into the probe even when the gas would have missed it).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Isokinetic Sampling** requires the velocity of gas entering the probe **Vₙ** to **exactly equal** the local gas velocity at the sampling point **Vₛ**. The angle of the probe nozzle must also be aligned **parallel** to the gas stream.

### 2.2.2 The KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Isokinetic Flow Rate | $Q_{iso} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4} \cdot V_s$ | m³/s | d = nozzle diameter, Vₛ = local stack gas velocity |
| 2 | Stack Gas Velocity (Pitot) | $V_s = C_p \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho_g}}$ | m/s | $C_p$ = Pitot coefficient ≈ 0.84 to 0.99 |
| 3 | Gas Density | $\rho_g = \dfrac{P \cdot M}{R \cdot T}$ | kg/m³ | M = molecular weight of wet gas |
| 4 | Volumetric Flow (Actual) | $Q_a = A_s \cdot V_s$ | m³/s | $A_s$ = cross-sectional area of stack |
| 5 | Standardisation to STP | $V_{std} = V_m \cdot \dfrac{P_m}{T_m} \cdot \dfrac{T_{std}}{P_{std}}$ | Nm³ | $T_{std}=273$ K, $P_{std}=101.325$ kPa |
| 6 | Particulate Concentration | $C_p = \dfrac{W_f - W_i}{V_{std}}$ | mg/Nm³ | Gravimetric basis |
| 7 | Moisture Content | $\%H_2O = \dfrac{V_{cond}}{V_{cond} + V_{gas}} \times 100$ | % | Impinger method |
| 8 | Reynolds (Probe) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho_g \cdot V_s \cdot d}{\mu}$ | – | Must be > 4000 for turbulence (sampling accuracy) |
| 9 | Sampling Train Correction | $\Delta H_{@iso} = 0.5 \cdot \rho_g \cdot V_s^2$ | Pa | Velocity pressure head |
| 10 | Saturated Gas Volume | $V_{sat} = V_{dry} \cdot \dfrac{P_{bar}}{P_{bar} - P_{w}}$ | m³ | Account for water vapor pressure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions for Isokinetic Validity:**
> - $0.95 \leq \dfrac{V_n}{V_s} \leq 1.05$ → acceptable tolerance band.
> - Nozzle diameter **d** typically 6–13 mm.
> - Probe must be **heated** above gas dew point to prevent condensation artifacts in the filter.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Regulatory Compliance** – Industries must demonstrate stack emissions ≤ consented limits.
- **Epidemiology** – Ambient data feeds models like **AERMOD / CALPUFF** to predict ground-level concentrations.
- **Control Equipment Sizing** – Stack sampling quantifies the *load* on ESPs, Baghouses, and Scrubbers.
- **Process Auditing** – Identifies whether combustion is efficient (high CO → poor combustion).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Isokinetic Sampling Flow Rate

The sampling train must withdraw gas at a rate such that the velocity at the nozzle inlet equals the stream velocity. By the principle of conservation of mass (incompressible flow approximation):

**Step 1:** Write the volumetric flow rate as the product of cross-sectional area of the nozzle and the inlet velocity.

$$
Q_{iso} = A_n \cdot V_n
$$

**Step 2:** For an isokinetic sample, set $V_n = V_s$.

$$
Q_{iso} = A_n \cdot V_s
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the geometric area of the circular nozzle, $A_n = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4}$.

$$
Q_{iso} = \frac{\pi \cdot d^2}{4} \cdot V_s
$$

**Step 4:** This is the flow rate that the **rotameter / orifice meter** downstream of the pump must indicate (corrected to the actual temperature and pressure at the meter).

> **Conversion Logic Note:** The meter reads flow at *meter conditions* (Tₘ, Pₘ). The true volumetric flow *at stack conditions* is different. We therefore standardize back to stack conditions using the ideal gas law.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Standardisation of Meter Volume to Stack Conditions

**Step 1:** Start with the ideal gas equation applied to both states (meter and standard/dry stack):

$$
\frac{P_m \cdot V_m}{T_m} = \frac{P_{std} \cdot V_{std}}{T_{std}}
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the standardized volume:

$$
V_{std} = V_m \cdot \frac{P_m}{T_m} \cdot \frac{T_{std}}{P_{std}}
$$

**Step 3:** Account for moisture. The dry gas fraction is $1 - B_{ws}$, where $B_{ws}$ is the moisture fraction by volume.

$$
V_{dry,std} = V_{std} \cdot \left(1 - B_{ws}\right)
$$

**Step 4:** Final pollutant concentration in the standard engineering unit (mg/Nm³):

$$
C_p = \frac{W_f - W_i}{V_{dry,std}}
$$

where $W_f - W_i$ is the net mass gain of the filter (initial – final mass), in mg.

> **Textual Conversion Logic:** The numerator is the *actual mass of dust captured* (gravimetric, post-lab conditioning in a desiccator at constant RH). The denominator is the *volume of dry gas that the mass represents*, normalized to 0 °C, 1 atm. This is the "**dry, standard cubic meter (Nm³)**" basis mandated by CPCB and USEPA.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choice of Sampling & Analytical Method

| Pollutant Class | Sampling Method | Analytical Method | Detection Limit / Sensitivity |
|-----------------|------------------|---------------------|-------------------------------|
| **TSP (Ambient)** | High Volume Sampler | Gravimetric (filter weigh) | ± 5 µg/m³ |
| **PM₁₀ / PM₂.₅** | RDS / Beta Attenuation | Gravimetric + Optical (optional) | ± 2 µg/m³ |
| **SO₂** | Bubbler in TCM | West-Gaeke Colorimetry (Pararosaniline) | 0.01 ppm |
| **NOₓ** | Bubbler in Saltzman reagent or direct | Saltzman / Chemiluminescence | 0.02 ppm |
| **CO** | Direct instrument (NDIR) | Non-Dispersive Infrared | 0.5 ppm |
| **O₃** | Direct instrument | UV Photometry / Chemiluminescence | 0.01 ppm |
| **VOCs** | Tedlar bag / Canister / Sorbent tube | GC-MS / FID / PID | ppt to ppb |
| **Pb in PM** | HVS filter | AAS / ICP-OES (after acid digestion) | 0.1 µg/m³ |
| **Stack Particulates** | USEPA Method 5 train | Gravimetric (filter + probe rinse) | 1 mg/Nm³ |
| **Stack SO₂** | USEPA Method 6 | Barium-Chloranilate titration | 1 ppm |
| **Stack NOₓ** | USEPA Method 7 | Phenol-Disulfonic Acid (PDA) / Chemiluminescence | 1 ppm |

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical (Typical KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A stack of diameter **1.2 m** carries flue gas at an average velocity of **10 m/s**. The sampling train uses a nozzle of diameter **8 mm**. Calculate:
(a) The isokinetic sampling flow rate.
(b) The total volumetric flow rate of flue gas.
(c) The required probe inlet area in mm².

**Solution:**

**Step (a):** Apply the isokinetic flow rate formula. The nozzle diameter in meters: $d = 8$ mm $= 0.008$ m.

$$
Q_{iso} = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.008)^2}{4} \cdot 10
$$

$$
Q_{iso} = \frac{\pi \cdot 6.4 \times 10^{-5}}{4} \cdot 10 = \frac{2.0106 \times 10^{-4}}{4} \cdot 10
$$

$$
Q_{iso} = 5.0265 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 10
$$

$$
Q_{iso} = 5.0265 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Convert to **L/min**:

$$
Q_{iso} = 5.0265 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^3/\text{s} \times 60 \times 1000 = 30.16\;\text{L/min}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** 1 m³ = 1000 L, and 1 minute = 60 s. Multiply to obtain a pumping rate that the operator sets on the rotameter.

**Step (b):** Total volumetric flow = Stack area × velocity.

$$
A_s = \frac{\pi \cdot (1.2)^2}{4} = 1.131 \;\text{m}^2
$$

$$
Q_{total} = 1.131 \cdot 10 = 11.31 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step (c):** Probe inlet area:

$$
A_n = \frac{\pi \cdot (8)^2}{4} = 50.27\;\text{mm}^2
$$

This falls within the standard EPA Method 5 nozzle range of **6–13 mm** (28.3–132.7 mm²), confirming the design is feasible.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 USEPA Method 5 Stack Sampling Train (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[STACK GAS] --> B[S-TYPE PITOT PROBE]
    B --> C[NOZZLE]
    C --> D[HEATED PROBE LINER]
    D --> E[FILTER HOLDER WITH GLASS FIBER FILTER]
    E --> F[HEATED CONDENSER / IMPINGER 1]
    F --> G[IMPJINGER 2 EMPTY]
    G --> H[SILICA GEL DRYER]
    H --> I[ORIFICE METER / ROTAMETER]
    I --> J[VACUUM PUMP]
    J --> K[GAS VOLUME METER]
    K --> L[EXHAUST]

    style A fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#b30000,color:#000000
    style B fill:#e5f2ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    style C fill:#e5f2ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    style D fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000000
    style E fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000000
    style F fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000000
    style G fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000000
    style H fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000000
    style I fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6,color:#000000
    style J fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000000
    style K fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6,color:#000000
    style L fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#666666,color:#000000
```

**Caption:** Functional block diagram of the **EPA Method 5 Source Sampling Train**. Particles are captured on the **filter (E)**; moisture is condensed in **Impingers 1 & 2 (F, G)**; remaining water vapor is removed in the **silica-gel dryer (H)**; dry gas volume is quantified by the **meter (K)** under controlled vacuum (J).

---

## 4.2 Ambient Air Quality Monitoring Station (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[AMBIENT ATMOSPHERE] --> N1[GASEOUS INLET MANIFOLD]
    M1 --> N2[PM10 PM2.5 INLET]

    N1 --> O1[SO2 ANALYZER UV FLUORESCENCE]
    N1 --> O2[NOX ANALYZER CHEMILUMINESCENCE]
    N1 --> O3[O3 ANALYZER UV PHOTOMETRY]
    N1 --> O4[CO ANALYZER NDIR]

    N2 --> P1[RDS PM10 SEPARATOR]
    P1 --> P2[PM10 FILTER]
    P1 --> P3[PM2.5 IMPACTOR]
    P3 --> P4[PM2.5 FILTER]

    P2 --> Q1[GRAVIMETRIC LAB]
    P4 --> Q1
    O1 --> R1[REAL TIME DATA LOGGER]
    O2 --> R1
    O3 --> R1
    O4 --> R1

    R1 --> S1[CONTROL ROOM DISPLAY]
    R1 --> S2[CPCB SERVER UPLINK]

    style M1 fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#b30000,color:#000000
    style Q1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000000
    style S1 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000000
    style S2 fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000000
```

**Caption:** Architecture of a **Continuous Ambient Air Quality Monitoring Station (CAAQMS)** showing the dual sampling paths for gaseous and particulate pollutants, with gravimetric and real-time instrumental analysis feeding into a centralized regulatory data pipeline.

---

## 4.3 Isokinetic Sampling Principle (Cause–Effect Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[STREAM VELOCITY Vs] --> B{PROBE VELOCITY Vn}
    B -- Vn EQUAL TO Vs --> C[ISOKINETIC CORRECT SAMPLE]
    B -- Vn GREATER THAN Vs --> D[FINE BIAS OVERSAMPLING OF FINES]
    B -- Vn LESS THAN Vs --> E[COARSE BIAS OVERSAMPLING OF COARSE]
    C --> F[REPRESENTATIVE PM CONCENTRATION]
    D --> G[ERRONEOUS LOW PM READINGS]
    E --> H[ERRONEOUS HIGH PM READINGS]

    style A fill:#e5f2ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    style C fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000000
    style D fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000000
    style E fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000000
    style F fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000000
    style G fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000000
    style H fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000000
```

**Caption:** The **Isokinetic Equilibrium Schematic** illustrating the three sampling regimes and their consequences on the representativeness of measured PM concentration.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define isokinetic sampling. Why is it essential in stack monitoring of particulate matter?**

**Model Answer:**
Isokinetic sampling is a technique in which the velocity of gas entering the sampling probe nozzle is maintained **equal** to the local gas velocity in the stack at the point of sampling. It is essential because, in the presence of inertial particulates, any mismatch between the probe inlet velocity and the stream velocity causes **selective bias** — fine particles are under-sampled when probe velocity is too high, and coarse particles are over-sampled when probe velocity is too low. Only at isokinetic conditions does the sample drawn into the nozzle faithfully represent the in-stack aerosol, enabling accurate calculation of emission concentration in **mg/Nm³**.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Briefly explain the principle of a Respirable Dust Sampler (RDS) and state the parameter it measures.**

**Model Answer:**
The Respirable Dust Sampler operates on the principle of **inertial size separation**. Air is drawn at ~1.1 m³/min through a **cyclonic pre-separator** or **impaction plate** that selectively removes particles with aerodynamic diameter **> 10 µm** (the inhalable-thoracic cutoff), since these are largely trapped in the human nasal passage and do not reach the lungs. The remaining fine fraction is collected on a **glass fiber filter** of size 20 cm × 25 cm. The parameter measured is **PM₁₀ (Particulate Matter ≤ 10 µm)**, expressed in µg/m³, determined gravimetrically by weighing the filter before and after sampling under controlled humidity.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 / Model Paper]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the components and working of a **High Volume Sampler (HVS)** used for ambient air quality monitoring of Total Suspended Particulates (TSP).

**(b) [7 Marks)** A high-volume sampler operates for **24 hours** at an average flow rate of **1.5 m³/min**. The initial mass of the filter is **3.4521 g** and the final mass is **3.5687 g**. Calculate the TSP concentration in **µg/m³** and compare it with the **CPCB NAAQS limit of 230 µg/m³ (24-hr)**.

#### Model Solution (a)

A High Volume Sampler consists of:

1. **Inlet hood** – a protective cap with a rain shield that admits ambient air and rejects particles > ~100 µm (insects, debris).
2. **Filter holder** – holds an 8″ × 10″ glass fiber filter of efficiency > 99% for 0.3 µm DOP smoke.
3. **Flow measurement device** – a rotameter or orifice meter calibrated to indicate flow in m³/min.
4. **Blower / motor** – a high-speed brushless motor that draws air through the filter.
5. **Timer / elapsed time meter** – records the duration of operation.
6. **Housing** – weatherproof metal cabinet.

**Working Principle:** Ambient air is drawn through the filter at a calibrated high flow rate (1.1–1.7 m³/min). The filter captures all suspended particulates. After 24 hours, the filter is conditioned in a desiccator for 24 h, then weighed. The mass difference gives the total particulates collected, and dividing by the total volume of air sampled (corrected to standard conditions) gives TSP in µg/m³.

#### Model Solution (b)

**Step 1:** Compute net mass of particulates collected.

$$
\Delta m = W_f - W_i = 3.5687 - 3.4521 = 0.1166 \;\text{g}
$$

Convert to micrograms:

$$
\Delta m = 0.1166 \times 10^6 = 1.166 \times 10^5 \;\mu\text{g} = 116{,}600 \;\mu\text{g}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute total volume of air sampled.

$$
V = Q \times t = 1.5 \;\text{m}^3/\text{min} \times 24 \times 60 \;\text{min} = 2160 \;\text{m}^3
$$

**Step 3:** Compute TSP concentration.

$$
C_{TSP} = \frac{\Delta m}{V} = \frac{116{,}600}{2160} = 53.98 \;\mu\text{g/m}^3
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** TSP = (mass gain, µg) / (total air volume, m³). Result must be compared in same units (µg/m³) as the standard.

**Step 4:** Comparison with CPCB NAAQS.

$$
C_{TSP} = 53.98 \;\mu\text{g/m}^3 \;\ll\; 230 \;\mu\text{g/m}^3
$$

**Conclusion:** The ambient TSP concentration is **well within the NAAQS 24-hour limit** (only ~23.5% of the permissible value), indicating compliance for the monitored location.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 / Model Paper – Alternative]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **EPA Method 5 stack sampling train** for measurement of particulate matter emissions from a stationary source.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A stack of diameter **0.8 m** carries gas at **12 m/s**. The sampling probe nozzle diameter is **6 mm**. Calculate: (i) the isokinetic flow rate, and (ii) the total volumetric flow rate in m³/h.

#### Model Solution (a)

**Components of EPA Method 5 Train (in order of gas flow):**

1. **S-Type Pitot Tube** – measures differential pressure (ΔP) for stack gas velocity using an S-shaped dual-leg design that minimizes pluggage.
2. **Nozzle** – a stainless steel calibrated orifice (6–13 mm) that defines the isokinetic flow rate.
3. **Heated Probe Liner** – maintained above water dew point (typically 120 °C) to prevent condensation of moisture on the filter.
4. **Filter Holder** – contains a pre-weighed **glass fiber filter** (binderless) that captures all particulates.
5. **Condenser / First Impinger** – chilled water-cooled impinger that condenses water vapor.
6. **Second Impinger** – empty or contains silica gel to remove residual moisture.
7. **Silica Gel Dryer** – absorbs remaining water vapor; provides the dry gas volume basis.
8. **Orifice Meter / Rotameter** – measures the metering flow rate.
9. **Vacuum Pump** – provides suction.
10. **Dry Gas Meter** – integrates total volume sampled at meter conditions (Tₘ, Pₘ).

**Procedure:** After sampling, the filter is conditioned in a desiccator for 24 hours and reweighed. The probe and nozzle are rinsed with acetone to collect particles deposited on internal walls. The particulate mass is the sum of **filter mass gain + rinse residue**. The volume of gas sampled is **corrected to dry standard conditions (0 °C, 1 atm)**. The concentration is then:

$$
C_p = \frac{\text{Particulate mass (mg)}}{\text{Volume of dry gas at STP (Nm}^3\text{)}}
$$

expressed in **mg/Nm³**.

#### Model Solution (b)

**Given:** Stack diameter $D_s = 0.8$ m, gas velocity $V_s = 12$ m/s, nozzle diameter $d_n = 6$ mm $= 0.006$ m.

**(i) Isokinetic flow rate:**

$$
Q_{iso} = \frac{\pi \cdot d_n^2}{4} \cdot V_s
$$

$$
Q_{iso} = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.006)^2}{4} \cdot 12
$$

$$
Q_{iso} = \frac{\pi \cdot 3.6 \times 10^{-5}}{4} \cdot 12
$$

$$
Q_{iso} = \frac{1.13097 \times 10^{-4}}{4} \cdot 12
$$

$$
Q_{iso} = 2.827 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 12
$$

$$
Q_{iso} = 3.393 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Convert to **L/min**:

$$
Q_{iso} = 3.393 \times 10^{-4} \times 1000 \times 60 = 20.36 \;\text{L/min}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** 1 m³ = 1000 L, 1 min = 60 s. The rotameter is set to this flow to maintain isokinetic conditions.

**(ii) Total volumetric flow rate:**

$$
A_s = \frac{\pi \cdot (0.8)^2}{4} = 0.5027 \;\text{m}^2
$$

$$
Q_{total} = A_s \cdot V_s = 0.5027 \cdot 12 = 6.032 \;\text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Convert to **m³/h**:

$$
Q_{total} = 6.032 \times 3600 = 21{,}716 \;\text{m}^3/\text{h} \;\approx\; 2.17 \times 10^4 \;\text{m}^3/\text{h}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Multiply by 3600 s/h to convert from m³/s. This value is the **total flue gas discharge rate**, used to compute total mass emission rate (kg/h) when multiplied by the PM concentration.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls – Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting to standardise volume to STP** (Nm³). Many students use sampling volume as-is, losing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Unit confusion** between ambient (µg/m³) and stack (mg/Nm³) – these are **not interchangeable**.
> 3. **Not accounting for moisture** in stack sampling – dry basis is mandatory.
> 4. **Missing the 0.95–1.05 isokinetic tolerance band** – must be stated explicitly for full credit.
> 5. **Failing to mention heating of probe** to avoid moisture condensation on the filter.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 1 – Topic 3):**

- **Ambient vs Stack:** Ambient measures *exposure*; Stack measures *source emission*. Both are essential and complementary.
- **HVS** – 1.1–1.7 m³/min, 8″×10″ filter, measures **TSP** (24-hr).
- **RDS** – 1.1 m³/min, cyclonic pre-separator, measures **PM₁₀** (and PM₂.₅ with extra impactor).
- **Isokinetic Sampling** – $V_n = V_s$. Tolerance: ±5%. **Non-isokinetic sampling = bias error.**
- **EPA Method 5 Train** – Pitot → Nozzle → Heated probe → Filter → Impingers → Dryer → Meter → Pump.
- **Standard Conditions** – $T_{std} = 273.15$ K, $P_{std} = 101.325$ kPa, **dry basis**.
- **PM concentration formula:** $C_p = \dfrac{W_f - W_i}{V_{std,\;dry}}$ in **mg/Nm³** (stack) or **µg/m³** (ambient).
- **Pitot tube** gives $V_s$ from $\Delta P$ and gas density. $C_p$ (Pitot coefficient) ≈ **0.84 – 0.99**.
- **Gaseous sampling:** SO₂ → West-Gaeke (TCM); NOₓ → Saltzman; O₃ → UV photometry; CO → NDIR; VOCs → GC-MS after sorbent tube / canister.
- **CPCB NAAQS (India) – 24-hr limits:** PM₁₀ = 100 µg/m³; PM₂.₅ = 60 µg/m³; SO₂ = 80 µg/m³; NO₂ = 80 µg/m³.
- **CPCB NAAQS (India) – Annual:** PM₁₀ = 60 µg/m³; PM₂.₅ = 40 µg/m³; SO₂ = 50 µg/m³; NO₂ = 40 µg/m³.
- **Probe heating** ≥ 120 °C to prevent moisture artifact in stack PM measurement.
- **Filter conditioning** – desiccate 24 h pre- and post-weighing at controlled RH (30–40%) and 15–30 °C.
- **Volumetric flow conversion:** $Q_{iso} \propto d^2 \cdot V_s$ – doubling the nozzle diameter **quadruples** the flow rate.
- **Sign convention in Pitot:** $V_s = C_p \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_g}}$; $\Delta P$ is the velocity pressure head, *not* the static pressure.
- **Anemometer (vane / hot-wire)** is for *ambient* wind; **Pitot tube** is for *in-stack* gas velocity measurement.
- **KTU 2024 Hot Topic:** Be prepared to differentiate **Method 5 (PM), Method 6 (SO₂), Method 7 (NOₓ), Method 8 (SO₃ + H₂SO₄ mist)** in the EPA source sampling series.
- **Sampling train leak check** is mandatory before and after every run; a leak rate > 0.02 CFM at 10 in. Hg invalidates the run.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effects of Meteorology on Air Pollution - Fundamentals, Atmospheric Stability - Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate Derivation, Inversion, Wind Profiles and Stack Plume Patterns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBT726 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Effects of Meteorology on Air Pollution - Fundamentals, Atmospheric Stability - Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate Derivation, Inversion, Wind Profiles and Stack Plume Patterns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Meteorology and Air Pollution — The Governing Link

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Meteorology** is the interdisciplinary science of the atmosphere, encompassing the study of temperature, pressure, wind, humidity, turbulence, and solar radiation. In the context of air pollution control, meteorology governs three critical functions: **(i)** the **transport** (advection) of pollutants away from the source, **(ii)** the **dispersion** (dilution) of pollutants into the surrounding atmosphere, and **(iii)** the **transformation** (chemical and physical) of pollutants during their atmospheric residence time.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Highlight:**
> Air pollution is NOT just about emission rate. Two identical factories with identical stacks can produce vastly different ground-level concentrations purely because of weather conditions. Meteorology acts as the **"atmospheric valve"** that controls whether pollutants accumulate to toxic levels or get harmlessly diluted.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Smoking Kitchen":**
> Imagine you are cooking in a small closed kitchen with a pressure cooker (the **source of pollution**). Now consider two scenarios:
> 1. **Scenario A (Stable Atmosphere):** You keep all windows and doors shut. The smoke and steam stay trapped inside — you suffocate. This is like an **inversion condition** where a lid of warm air traps pollutants.
> 2. **Scenario B (Unstable Atmosphere):** You open the windows AND switch on the exhaust fan. Air rushes in from below and escapes from above — the kitchen clears rapidly. This is like a **convective, unstable atmosphere** with good vertical mixing.
>
> The cooker output is the same in both cases — but your "breathing experience" is dramatically different. **Meteorology decides whether your city is "Scenario A" or "Scenario B".**

---

### 1.3 Key Atmospheric Parameters and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Physical Constants & Standard Reference Values (Bold for emphasis):**

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | **g** | **9.81 m/s²** | Drives buoyancy & hydrostatic pressure |
| Specific heat of dry air at constant pressure | **Cp** | **1005 J/(kg·K)** | Controls adiabatic heating/cooling rate |
| Specific gas constant for dry air | **R** | **287 J/(kg·K)** | Links P, V, T of air |
| Universal gas constant | **R̄** | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | Used in molar calculations |
| Molecular weight of dry air | **M** | **28.97 g/mol** | For density calculations |
| Standard sea-level temperature | **T₀** | **288.15 K (15 °C)** | Reference for standard atmosphere |
| Standard sea-level pressure | **P₀** | **101.325 kPa** | Reference atmospheric pressure |
| von Kármán constant | **κ** | **0.41** | Used in logarithmic wind profile |

---

### 1.4 The Three "Meteorological Levers" on Pollution

> [!NOTE]
> **1. Transport (Wind Speed & Direction):**
> Determines WHERE pollutants go. A 5 m/s wind disperses pollutants differently than a 0.5 m/s wind — wind speed is in the denominator of most ground-level concentration formulas.
>
> **2. Dispersion (Atmospheric Stability):**
> Determines HOW MUCH vertical mixing occurs. Stability is dictated by the relative temperature gradient of the atmosphere versus the adiabatic lapse rate.
>
> **3. Transformation (Solar Radiation, Humidity, Temperature):**
> Determines WHETHER pollutants chemically change (e.g., NO₂ → O₃ photolysis, SO₂ → H₂SO₄ aerosol).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Atmospheric Thermodynamic Foundation

The atmosphere is a **compressible fluid** bound to Earth by gravity. Three governing equations form its thermodynamic backbone:

#### (A) Ideal Gas Law (Equation of State)

$$p = \rho R T$$

Where:
- $p$ = absolute pressure (Pa)
- $\rho$ = air density (kg/m³)
- $R$ = specific gas constant for dry air = **287 J/(kg·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** This equation tells us that warm air is less dense than cold air (at the same pressure). This single fact drives ALL buoyancy-driven atmospheric motion, including plume rise and inversion formation.

#### (B) Hydrostatic Equation

The vertical pressure gradient is dictated by the weight of the air column above:

$$\frac{dp}{dz} = -\rho g$$

This is derived by force balance on an infinitesimal air parcel: the upward pressure force at the bottom minus the downward pressure force at the top equals the weight of the parcel.

#### (C) First Law of Thermodynamics

For an air parcel undergoing a process:

$$\delta q = du + \delta w$$

For a reversible process in a closed system: $\delta w = p \, dv$, and $du = C_v \, dT$. For an ideal gas with constant specific heats:

$$\delta q = C_v \, dT + p \, dv$$

---

### 2.2 Adiabatic Processes — Why "Adiabatic" Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Adiabatic Process:**
> An **adiabatic process** is one in which **no heat is exchanged with the surroundings** ($\delta q = 0$). In the atmosphere, an unsaturated air parcel that rises or sinks rapidly enough (so that conductive heat exchange with surroundings is negligible) undergoes an **adiabatic transformation**.

**Why is this critical for air pollution?**
- A rising pollutant plume (warm exhaust) behaves as an **adiabatically expanding** parcel.
- A descending air mass (subsidence) behaves as an **adiabatically compressing** parcel.
- The temperature change with height of such a parcel defines the **Adiabatic Lapse Rate** — the benchmark against which atmospheric stability is judged.

---

### 2.3 The Three Lapse Rates — The Holy Trinity of Stability

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Lapse Rates Every KTU Student MUST Memorize:**

| Lapse Rate | Symbol | Governing Equation | Typical Magnitude | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate** | **Γd** (or DALR) | $\Gamma_d = g/C_p$ | **≈ 9.8 K/km (≈ 1 °C per 100 m)** | Temp change of unsaturated rising parcel |
| **Saturated (Wet) Adiabatic Lapse Rate** | **Γs** (or SALR) | $\Gamma_s = \Gamma_d \cdot \dfrac{1 + \dfrac{L_v \, w_s}{R \, T}}{1 + \dfrac{L_v^2 \, w_s \, \varepsilon}{C_p \, R \, T^2}}$ | **≈ 4–6 K/km** (varies with T) | Temp change of saturated rising parcel (latent heat released) |
| **Environmental Lapse Rate** | **Γ** (or ELR) | $\Gamma = -\dfrac{dT}{dz}\Big|_{observed}$ | **≈ 3–9.8 K/km** (highly variable) | Actual observed atmospheric temp profile |

Where:
- $L_v$ = latent heat of vaporization of water ≈ **2.5 × 10⁶ J/kg**
- $w_s$ = saturation mixing ratio
- $\varepsilon = R_d/R_v \approx 0.622$

---

### 2.4 Atmospheric Stability Criteria — The Decision Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Stability Rule (Board-Favorite Question):**

| Condition | Stability Class | Physical Consequence | Plume Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\Gamma > \Gamma_d$ (ELR > DALR) | **Absolutely UNSTABLE** | Any displaced parcel keeps moving | Strong vertical mixing, looping plume |
| $\Gamma = \Gamma_d$ | **Neutral** | Parcel neither rises nor sinks | Coning plume |
| $\Gamma_s < \Gamma < \Gamma_d$ | **Conditionally Unstable** | Stable if dry, unstable if saturated | Conditional behavior |
| $\Gamma < \Gamma_s < \Gamma_d$ | **Absolutely STABLE** | Any displaced parcel returns | Fanning plume, poor dispersion |
| $\Gamma < 0$ (T increases with z) | **Inversion** | Extreme stability — temperature increases with height | Trapping, fumigation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Implication:**
> Design of stack height must consider the **worst-case stability scenario** (usually D-class stability with low wind speed), which produces maximum ground-level concentration.

---

### 2.5 Pasquill-Gifford Stability Classification (A–F)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Table — Pasquill-Gifford Stability Classes:**

| Stability Class | Description | Conditions | Dispersion Quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | Very Unstable | Strong insolation, low wind | Excellent (worst-case for ground concentration due to looping) |
| **B** | Unstable | Moderate insolation | Very good |
| **C** | Slightly Unstable | Slight insolation | Good |
| **D** | **Neutral** | Overcast, moderate-to-high wind | Moderate (default design class) |
| **E** | Slightly Stable | Light wind, clear night, thin cloud | Poor |
| **F** | **Very Stable** | Calm wind, clear night | Very poor (worst for inversions) |

---

### 2.6 Wind Profile Power Law

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Formula (Used in Plume Rise & Dispersion Models):**

$$\frac{u(z)}{u(z_r)} = \left(\frac{z}{z_r}\right)^p$$

Where:
- $u(z)$ = wind speed at height $z$
- $u(z_r)$ = wind speed at reference height $z_r$ (usually 10 m)
- $p$ = **exponent depending on stability class**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability-Dependent Exponent p (KTU Must-Know):**

| Stability Class | A | B | C | D | E | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **p** | 0.07 | 0.07 | 0.10 | 0.15 | 0.35 | 0.55 |

**Larger p = more stable = greater wind shear (wind increases more rapidly with height).**

---

### 2.7 Logarithmic Wind Profile (Theoretical Foundation)

For **neutral conditions** over rough terrain:

$$u(z) = \frac{u_*}{\kappa} \ln\left(\frac{z}{z_0}\right)$$

Where:
- $u_*$ = friction velocity (m/s)
- $\kappa$ = von Kármán constant = **0.41**
- $z_0$ = surface roughness length (m)
  - Smooth surfaces (mud flats, snow): $z_0 \approx 0.001$ m
  - Grass: $z_0 \approx 0.01$–0.05 m
  - Forests, urban areas: $z_0 \approx 1$–5 m

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:**
> The logarithmic profile is more physically rigorous and is used in **advanced Gaussian plume models** (e.g., AERMOD) when terrain roughness matters. The power law is an empirical simplification commonly used in EPA's **ISCST3** model.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Complete Derivation of the Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate (DALR)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Derive the expression $\Gamma_d = -\dfrac{dT}{dz} = \dfrac{g}{C_p}$ for an unsaturated air parcel undergoing adiabatic ascent/descent.

---

#### STEP 1 — Start with the First Law of Thermodynamics for a Reversible Process

For an ideal gas undergoing a reversible process in a closed system (no mass transfer):

$$dq = du + dw$$

Where:
- $dq$ = infinitesimal heat added to the system
- $du$ = infinitesimal change in internal energy
- $dw$ = infinitesimal work done BY the system

---

#### STEP 2 — Substitute the Definitions for Internal Energy and Work

For an ideal gas, the change in internal energy depends only on temperature change:

$$du = C_v \, dT$$

For a reversible process, the work done by the system is:

$$dw = p \, dv$$

Substituting into the First Law:

$$dq = C_v \, dT + p \, dv$$

---

#### STEP 3 — Apply the Adiabatic Condition

For an adiabatic process, **no heat is exchanged** with the surroundings:

$$dq = 0$$

Therefore:

$$0 = C_v \, dT + p \, dv$$

Rearranging:

$$C_v \, dT = -p \, dv \quad \quad \text{...(Equation A)}$$

---

#### STEP 4 — Differentiate the Ideal Gas Law

Starting from:

$$p \, v = R \, T$$

Differentiating both sides (treating all three variables $p, v, T$ as changing):

$$p \, dv + v \, dp = R \, dT$$

Solving for $p \, dv$:

$$p \, dv = R \, dT - v \, dp \quad \quad \text{...(Equation B)}$$

---

#### STEP 5 — Substitute Equation B into Equation A

$$C_v \, dT = -(R \, dT - v \, dp)$$

$$C_v \, dT = -R \, dT + v \, dp$$

Rearranging terms:

$$C_v \, dT + R \, dT = v \, dp$$

$$(C_v + R) \, dT = v \, dp$$

---

#### STEP 6 — Use Mayer's Relation

> [!NOTE]
> **Mayer's Relation:** For an ideal gas, $C_p - C_v = R$, which implies $C_p = C_v + R$.

Therefore:

$$C_p \, dT = v \, dp \quad \quad \text{...(Equation C)}$$

---

#### STEP 7 — Eliminate Specific Volume Using Ideal Gas Law

From the ideal gas law:

$$v = \frac{R \, T}{p}$$

Substituting into Equation C:

$$C_p \, dT = \frac{R \, T}{p} \, dp$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dT}{T} = \frac{R}{C_p} \cdot \frac{dp}{p}$$

This is the **Poisson Equation** — the fundamental relation for adiabatic processes in an ideal gas.

---

#### STEP 8 — Apply the Hydrostatic Equation

The hydrostatic equation gives:

$$dp = -\rho \, g \, dz$$

Using the ideal gas law to eliminate density ($\rho = p/(RT)$):

$$dp = -\frac{p}{R \, T} \cdot g \, dz$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{dp}{p} = -\frac{g}{R \, T} \, dz$$

---

#### STEP 9 — Substitute into the Poisson Equation

$$\frac{dT}{T} = \frac{R}{C_p} \cdot \left(-\frac{g}{R \, T} \, dz\right)$$

The $R$ in numerator and denominator cancel, and the $T$ in denominator cancels with one in the numerator:

$$\frac{dT}{T} = -\frac{g}{C_p} \cdot \frac{dz}{T}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{dT}{dz} = -\frac{g}{C_p}$$

---

#### STEP 10 — Final Result and Numerical Evaluation

$$\boxed{\Gamma_d = -\frac{dT}{dz} = \frac{g}{C_p}}$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$\Gamma_d = \frac{9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2}{1005 \, \text{J/(kg·K)}} = 9.761 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{K/m}$$

Converting to more intuitive units:

$$\Gamma_d = 9.76 \, \text{K/km} \approx 9.8 \, \text{K/km} \approx 1 \, \text{°C per 100 \, m}$$

---

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Stability Determination

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A meteorological tower records the following temperature profile:
> - At z = 0 m: T = 20 °C
> - At z = 100 m: T = 19 °C
> - At z = 500 m: T = 16 °C
> - At z = 1000 m: T = 14 °C
>
> Determine the stability class and comment on pollutant dispersion behavior.

---

#### STEP 1 — Calculate the Environmental Lapse Rate (ELR)

From the surface to 1000 m:

$$\Gamma_{ELR} = -\frac{dT}{dz} = -\frac{(14 - 20) \, \text{°C}}{(1000 - 0) \, \text{m}} = \frac{6 \, \text{K}}{1000 \, \text{m}} = 6 \, \text{K/km}$$

---

#### STEP 2 — Compare with DALR

$$\Gamma_{ELR} = 6 \, \text{K/km} \quad < \quad \Gamma_d = 9.8 \, \text{K/km}$$

---

#### STEP 3 — Apply Stability Criterion

Since $\Gamma_{ELR} < \Gamma_d$, the actual atmosphere cools **more slowly** with height than an adiabatic parcel would. This means:

- If a parcel is pushed upward, it cools at 9.8 K/km (faster than surroundings at 6 K/km).
- After rising 100 m, the parcel is COLDER than its surroundings.
- Cold parcel is DENSER and SINKS back.
- **Therefore, the atmosphere is STABLE.**

---

#### STEP 4 — Classify Using Pasquill-Gifford

Based on the moderate lapse rate (6 K/km), overcast conditions, and moderate wind — this corresponds to **Pasquill Class D (Neutral)** to **Class E (Slightly Stable)**.

**Engineering Implication:** Stack height must be designed so that plume rise $\Delta H$ + stack height $H_s$ places the plume above the stable layer's base, or alternatively, the design must account for limited vertical mixing using Gaussian plume stability corrections.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Power Law Wind Profile Exponent

> [!NOTE]
> **Empirical Justification (Simplified):**
> The power law exponent $p$ is related to the Monin-Obukhov length $L$ (a measure of stability). For neutral conditions, $p \approx 0.15$. For stable conditions, $p$ increases because the boundary layer is shallower and wind shear is concentrated over a smaller depth.

For unstable conditions, the logarithmic profile is modified using the dimensionless stability parameter $\zeta = z/L$:

$$u(z) = \frac{u_*}{\kappa}\left[\ln\left(\frac{z}{z_0}\right) - \Psi_m\left(\frac{z}{L}\right)\right]$$

Where $\Psi_m$ is the **momentum stability function** (Businger-Dyer form). The power law approximation:

$$\boxed{p = \frac{1}{\ln(z/z_0)}\left[0.35 + \frac{0.71 \cdot z}{L}\right]^{1/2}}$$

For neutral conditions ($L \to \infty$):

$$p \approx \frac{0.35^{1/2}}{\ln(z/z_0)} \approx \frac{0.59}{\ln(z/z_0)} \approx 0.15 \text{ (for typical urban } z_0 \approx 1 \text{ m)}$$

---

### 3.4 Stack Plume Rise — The Holland Equation (Meteorology in Action)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Holland's Plume Rise Formula (KTU Favorite):**

$$\Delta H = \frac{v_s \, d}{u} \left(1.5 + 2.68 \times 10^{-3} \, P \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{T_s \, d}\right)$$

Where:
- $\Delta H$ = plume rise above stack top (m)
- $v_s$ = stack gas exit velocity (m/s)
- $d$ = stack diameter (m)
- $u$ = wind speed at stack height (m/s)
- $P$ = atmospheric pressure (mbar or hPa)
- $\Delta T = T_s - T_a$ (difference between stack gas and ambient temperature, K)
- $T_s$ = stack gas temperature (K)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that $\Delta H$ is **inversely proportional** to wind speed $u$. This means **high winds reduce plume rise**, potentially trapping pollutants near the ground. This is why pollutant concentrations peak during **moderate winds + stable conditions** — not during calm or stormy weather.

---

### 3.5 Maximum Ground-Level Concentration Formula

For the standard Gaussian plume model with reflection from the ground:

$$\boxed{C_{max} = \frac{2.032 \, Q}{u \, H_e^2} \cdot \frac{\sigma_z}{\sigma_y}}$$

Where:
- $C_{max}$ = maximum ground-level concentration (g/m³)
- $Q$ = emission rate (g/s)
- $u$ = wind speed at stack height (m/s)
- $H_e = H_s + \Delta H$ = effective stack height (m)
- $\sigma_y, \sigma_z$ = horizontal and vertical dispersion coefficients (functions of downwind distance $x$ and stability class)

**The Critical Distance** at which $C_{max}$ occurs:

$$x_{max} = \frac{H_e}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{\sigma_z}{\sigma_y}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q: Define the Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate (DALR). State its numerical value and explain its significance in determining atmospheric stability.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation Shown):**

> **(1 Mark)** **Definition:** The Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate is the rate at which the temperature of an unsaturated air parcel changes with height when it is displaced vertically under adiabatic conditions (no heat exchange with surroundings). It is given by:
>
> $$\Gamma_d = -\frac{dT}{dz} = \frac{g}{C_p}$$
>
> **(1 Mark)** **Numerical Value:** Substituting $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ and $C_p = 1005 \, \text{J/(kg·K)}$:
>
> $$\Gamma_d = \frac{9.81}{1005} = 9.76 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{K/m} \approx 9.8 \, \text{K/km}$$
>
> This is approximately **1 °C per 100 m** of vertical ascent.
>
> **(1 Mark)** **Significance in Stability:** The DALR is the **reference benchmark** against which the actual environmental lapse rate (ELR) is compared:
> - If $\Gamma_{ELR} > \Gamma_d$: atmosphere is **unstable**.
> - If $\Gamma_{ELR} < \Gamma_d$: atmosphere is **stable**.
> - If $\Gamma_{ELR} = \Gamma_d$: atmosphere is **neutral**.

---

#### **Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q: Differentiate between temperature inversion and lapse rate. List any two types of inversions.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation Shown):**

> **(1 Mark)** **Lapse Rate:** It is the rate at which atmospheric temperature decreases with increasing altitude under normal conditions. The standard atmospheric lapse rate is approximately **6.5 K/km**.
>
> **(1 Mark)** **Temperature Inversion:** It is the condition in which temperature **increases** with altitude (instead of decreasing), i.e., the lapse rate is **negative**. This creates an extremely stable layer that acts as a "lid" preventing vertical mixing of pollutants.
>
> **(2 Marks — Any two types):**
> 1. **Radiation Inversion:** Formed on clear, calm nights when the ground loses heat by longwave radiation, cooling the air near the surface below the warmer air aloft.
> 2. **Subsidence Inversion:** Formed when a large mass of air descends and is compressed adiabatically, warming the upper layers.
> 3. **Frontal Inversion:** Occurs at the boundary between a cold air mass and an overriding warm air mass at a weather front.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **Dry Adiabatic Lapse Rate (DALR)** from the fundamental thermodynamic principles. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A stack emits gases at a velocity of $v_s = 12 \, \text{m/s}$ through a circular opening of diameter $d = 1.5 \, \text{m}$. The stack gas temperature is $T_s = 410 \, \text{K}$, ambient air temperature is $T_a = 300 \, \text{K}$, atmospheric pressure is $P = 1010 \, \text{mbar}$, and wind speed at stack height is $u = 5 \, \text{m/s}$. Calculate the **effective stack height** using Holland's formula if the actual stack height is $H_s = 80 \, \text{m}$. Comment on the plume behavior under **D-class stability**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a): Derivation of DALR (7 Marks)**

> **Step 1 (1 Mark) — First Law of Thermodynamics:**
> For a reversible process in a closed ideal gas system:
>
> $$dq = C_v \, dT + p \, dv$$
>
> **Step 2 (1 Mark) — Apply Adiabatic Condition:**
> For an adiabatic process, $dq = 0$:
>
> $$C_v \, dT = -p \, dv \quad \text{...(1)}$$
>
> **Step 3 (1 Mark) — Differentiate Ideal Gas Law:**
> From $pv = RT$, differentiate both sides:
>
> $$p \, dv + v \, dp = R \, dT$$
>
> $$p \, dv = R \, dT - v \, dp \quad \text{...(2)}$$
>
> **Step 4 (1 Mark) — Substitute (2) into (1):**
>
> $$C_v \, dT = -(R \, dT - v \, dp) = -R \, dT + v \, dp$$
>
> $$C_v \, dT + R \, dT = v \, dp$$
>
> **Step 5 (1 Mark) — Apply Mayer's Relation $C_p = C_v + R$:**
>
> $$C_p \, dT = v \, dp$$
>
> **Step 6 (1 Mark) — Hydrostatic Equation:**
>
> $$dp = -\rho \, g \, dz = -\frac{p}{RT} g \, dz$$
>
> Substituting and rearranging:
>
> $$C_p \, dT = \frac{RT}{p} \cdot \left(-\frac{p}{RT}\right) g \, dz = -g \, dz$$
>
> **Step 7 (1 Mark) — Final Result:**
>
> $$\frac{dT}{dz} = -\frac{g}{C_p}$$
>
> $$\boxed{\Gamma_d = -\frac{dT}{dz} = \frac{g}{C_p} = \frac{9.81}{1005} \approx 9.8 \, \text{K/km}}$$

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b): Holland's Plume Rise (7 Marks)**

> **Step 1 (1 Mark) — State Holland's Formula:**
>
> $$\Delta H = \frac{v_s \, d}{u}\left(1.5 + 2.68 \times 10^{-3} \cdot P \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{T_s \, d}\right)$$
>
> **Step 2 (1 Mark) — Identify Given Values:**
> - $v_s = 12 \, \text{m/s}$
> - $d = 1.5 \, \text{m}$
> - $u = 5 \, \text{m/s}$
> - $P = 1010 \, \text{mbar}$
> - $T_s = 410 \, \text{K}$
> - $T_a = 300 \, \text{K}$
> - $\Delta T = T_s - T_a = 410 - 300 = 110 \, \text{K}$
>
> **Step 3 (1 Mark) — Compute the First Term:**
>
> $$\frac{v_s \, d}{u} = \frac{12 \times 1.5}{5} = \frac{18}{5} = 3.6 \, \text{m}$$
>
> **Step 4 (2 Marks) — Compute the Buoyancy Term:**
>
> $$\frac{\Delta T}{T_s \, d} = \frac{110}{410 \times 1.5} = \frac{110}{615} = 0.1789 \, \text{m}^{-1}$$
>
> $$2.68 \times 10^{-3} \cdot P \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{T_s \, d} = 2.68 \times 10^{-3} \times 1010 \times 0.1789$$
>
> $$= 2.7068 \times 0.1789 = 0.4842$$
>
> **Step 5 (1 Mark) — Calculate Plume Rise:**
>
> $$\Delta H = 3.6 \times (1.5 + 0.4842) = 3.6 \times 1.9842 = 7.14 \, \text{m}$$
>
> **Step 6 (1 Mark) — Effective Stack Height:**
>
> $$H_e = H_s + \Delta H = 80 + 7.14 = 87.14 \, \text{m}$$
>
> **Plume Behavior Under D-Class Stability:**
> Under Pasquill Class D (neutral) conditions, the plume exhibits a **coning pattern** — symmetric lateral and vertical spreading with neither strong updrafts nor suppression. Dispersion is moderate, and the plume follows a balanced conical shape downwind.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Define atmospheric stability. With the help of neat sketches, explain the **six major plume behavior patterns** observed from industrial stacks. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The following data was recorded from a meteorological tower at an industrial site:
> - Surface temperature: $T_0 = 28 \, ^\circ\text{C}$
> - Temperature at 500 m: $T_{500} = 22 \, ^\circ\text{C}$
> - Temperature at 1000 m: $T_{1000} = 18 \, ^\circ\text{C}$
>
> **(i)** Calculate the Environmental Lapse Rate (ELR).
> **(ii)** Determine the stability class and justify.
> **(iii)** If the wind speed at 10 m height is 3 m/s, estimate the wind speed at 100 m height for D-class stability. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a): Plume Patterns (7 Marks)**

> **(1 Mark) Definition:**
> **Atmospheric stability** is the resistance of the atmosphere to vertical motion. A stable atmosphere suppresses vertical mixing (pollutants accumulate); an unstable atmosphere enhances vertical mixing (pollutants disperse rapidly).
>
> **(6 Marks — Six Plume Patterns — 1 Mark Each):**
>
> **1. Looping Plume:** Occurs under very unstable (A) or unstable (B) conditions with light winds. Strong convective eddies cause the plume to loop up and down. Ground concentration is low due to rapid vertical mixing.
>
> **2. Coning Plume:** Occurs under neutral (D) conditions with moderate-to-strong winds. The plume spreads symmetrically in a cone shape. Provides moderate dispersion.
>
> **3. Fanning Plume:** Occurs under very stable (F) conditions with light winds and an inversion layer ABOVE the stack. Plume spreads horizontally with negligible vertical mixing.
>
> **4. Fumigation Plume:** The most dangerous condition. An inversion exists BELOW the stack top; the plume is trapped in the convective mixed layer beneath the inversion lid. Causes extremely high ground-level concentrations.
>
> **5. Lofting Plume:** Inversion exists BELOW the stack top only; the plume rises freely upward with minimal downward mixing. Best-case scenario for ground-level pollution.
>
> **6. Trapping Plume:** Inversion layers exist both ABOVE and BELOW the stack top; the plume is confined laterally within a narrow band. Causes elevated ground concentrations over a wide area.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b): Stability & Wind Profile (7 Marks)**

> **(i) Environmental Lapse Rate (2 Marks):**
>
> From surface to 1000 m:
>
> $$\Gamma_{ELR} = -\frac{dT}{dz} = -\frac{(18 - 28) \, ^\circ\text{C}}{(1000 - 0) \, \text{m}} = \frac{10 \, \text{K}}{1000 \, \text{m}} = 10 \, \text{K/km}$$
>
> **(ii) Stability Class Determination (3 Marks):**
>
> Compare with DALR $= 9.8 \, \text{K/km}$:
>
> $$\Gamma_{ELR} = 10 \, \text{K/km} > \Gamma_d = 9.8 \, \text{K/km}$$
>
> Since $\Gamma_{ELR} > \Gamma_d$, the atmosphere is **ABSOLUTELY UNSTABLE**.
>
> Considering overcast/moderate conditions, this corresponds to **Pasquill Class C (Slightly Unstable)**. Vertical mixing is enhanced; plume exhibits coning-to-looping behavior.
>
> **(iii) Wind Speed at 100 m for D-class Stability (2 Marks):**
>
> Power law:
>
> $$\frac{u(z)}{u(z_r)} = \left(\frac{z}{z_r}\right)^p$$
>
> For Class D: $p = 0.15$:
>
> $$u(100) = 3 \times \left(\frac{100}{10}\right)^{0.15} = 3 \times (10)^{0.15}$$
>
> Using $\log(10) = 1$, $(10)^{0.15} = 10^{0.15} \approx 1.413$:
>
> $$u(100) = 3 \times 1.413 = 4.24 \, \text{m/s}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> **Pitfall 1 (–1 Mark):** Confusing **Environmental Lapse Rate** with **Adiabatic Lapse Rate**. ELR is OBSERVED; DALR is THEORETICAL for an adiabatic parcel.
>
> **Pitfall 2 (–2 Marks):** Forgetting to **convert temperature differences to Kelvin** when applying DALR formula. Always ensure $\Delta T$ is in K, not °C (though the magnitude is identical, examiners check the unit).
>
> **Pitfall 3 (–2 Marks):** In plume rise calculation, students often use **°C instead of Kelvin** for $T_s$ in the denominator of Holland's formula. This produces wrong answers.
>
> **Pitfall 4 (–1 Mark):** Failing to state the **stability criterion direction correctly**: $\Gamma_{ELR} > \Gamma_d$ = UNSTABLE; $\Gamma_{ELR} < \Gamma_d$ = STABLE. Many students invert this.
>
> **Pitfall 5 (–1 Mark):** In wind profile calculations, students often use **wrong exponent p for the stability class**. Memorize: A,B = 0.07; C = 0.10; D = 0.15; E = 0.35; F = 0.55.
>
> **Pitfall 6 (–1 Mark):** Failing to draw sketches for plume patterns — KTU examiners **expect 6 small diagrams** for the 7-mark question; missing sketches can lose 2-3 marks easily.

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-DENSITY SUMMARY — Read This Before Every Exam:**

- ✅ **DALR Formula:** $\Gamma_d = g/C_p = 9.8 \, \text{K/km} \approx 1 \, ^\circ\text{C}/100\,\text{m}$
- ✅ **Stability Rule:** ELR > DALR → UNSTABLE; ELR < DALR → STABLE; ELR = DALR → NEUTRAL
- ✅ **Inversion = Negative ELR** (temperature increases with height) — worst-case for pollution
- ✅ **Inversion Types:** Radiation, Subsidence, Frontal, Advection (Marine)
- ✅ **Power Law:** $u(z)/u(z_r) = (z/z_r)^p$; exponents: A,B=0.07, C=0.10, D=0.15, E=0.35, F=0.55
- ✅ **Logarithmic Law (Neutral):** $u(z) = (u_*/\kappa) \ln(z/z_0)$; $\kappa = 0.41$
- ✅ **Six Plume Patterns:** Looping, Coning, Fanning, Fumigation, Lofting, Trapping
- ✅ **Worst Plume for Ground Pollution:** FUMIGATION (inversion below stack)
- ✅ **Best Plume for Ground Pollution:** LOFTING (inversion below stack, plume escapes upward)
- ✅ **Pasquill Classes:** A (very unstable), D (neutral — default design), F (very stable)
- ✅ **Holland's Plume Rise:** $\Delta H = (v_s d/u) \cdot [1.5 + 2.68 \times 10^{-3} \cdot P \cdot \Delta T/(T_s d)]$
- ✅ **Effective Stack Height:** $H_e = H_s + \Delta H$
- ✅ **Maximum Ground Concentration Occurs at:** $x_{max} = H_e \sqrt{2} \cdot \sigma_z/\sigma_y$
- ✅ **Plume Rise is INVERSELY proportional to wind speed** — moderate winds give worst-case ground concentration
- ✅ **Design Stack for:** Class D stability (default), wind speed 5–6 m/s typically, ensure plume clears inversion lid
- ✅ **Mayer's Relation:** $C_p - C_v = R$ (always use this in derivations)
- ✅ **Hydrostatic Equation:** $dp/dz = -\rho g$ (the foundation of all atmospheric lapse rate derivations)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Atmospheric Diffusion Theories - Dispersion Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBT726 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Atmospheric Diffusion Theories - Dispersion Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Atmospheric Diffusion — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Atmospheric Diffusion** is the physical process by which airborne pollutants emitted from a source are transported and scattered through the surrounding atmosphere by the action of **turbulent air motion (eddies)**, molecular diffusion, and wind advection. It governs the spatial and temporal spreading of contaminants downwind of an emission source.

**Dispersion Models** are mathematically formulated tools that quantitatively predict the **downwind concentration** of pollutants as a function of:
- Emission rate of the source ($Q$)
- Meteorological parameters (wind speed $u$, stability class, mixing height)
- Physical parameters (effective stack height $H$, receptor coordinates $x, y, z$)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"Ink Drop in a Fish Tank" Analogy** — Imagine you drop a single drop of ink into a still fish tank. The ink doesn't stay as a dot; it spreads outward in a *cone-like cloud*. Now imagine a tap continuously dripping ink while a steady current (the wind) pushes it sideways. The ink forms a *plume* that gets wider and more dilute the further downstream you look.
>
> - The **width** of the plume depends on how **turbulent** the water (air) is.
> - A **strong, steady current** keeps the plume narrow and concentrated.
> - **Still water** allows ink to diffuse slowly in all directions.
>
> Atmospheric diffusion models are mathematical "fish-tank simulations" predicting where the ink (pollutant) will be at any distance downstream.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Wind speed ($u$):** measured in **m/s**, typically at stack height (10 m reference).
- **Emission rate ($Q$):** in **g/s** or **µg/s**.
- **Concentration ($C$):** in **µg/m³** or **ppm**.
- **Effective stack height ($H = h_s + \Delta h$):** in **meters**, where $h_s$ is physical stack height and $\Delta h$ is plume rise.
- **Standard deviation of plume spread ($\sigma_y, \sigma_z$):** in **meters** — direct measures of horizontal and vertical diffusion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Module-2 syllabus explicitly requires understanding the **Gaussian Plume Model**, **Sutton's Diffusion Theory**, and **Pasquill-Gifford Stability Classification**. These three form the conceptual backbone of all regulatory dispersion modeling (e.g., **ISCST3, AERMOD, CALPUFF**).

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Gaussian Plume Dispersion Model — Conceptual Logic

The Gaussian Plume Model (also called the **Steady-State Plume Model**) is the most widely used analytical dispersion model in air quality management. It is built on the following assumptions:

1. **Steady-state emissions:** The pollutant is released at a **constant rate $Q$**.
2. **Steady, uniform wind:** Wind speed $u$ is constant in space and time along the $x$-axis.
3. **Turbulent diffusion:** The concentration distribution in the crosswind ($y$) and vertical ($z$) directions follows a **Gaussian (Normal) probability distribution**.
4. **No chemical reaction or deposition:** Pollutant is conservative (except for ground reflection term).
5. **Ground-level reflection:** The ground acts as a perfect *reflector* of the plume — captured mathematically by an **image source** at $z = -H$.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Gaussian Assumption:** Turbulent eddies of many sizes act on the plume. By the **Central Limit Theorem** (Statistics), the *sum of many independent random motions* tends toward a Gaussian distribution. This is why pollutant concentration spreads as a bell-curve in $y$ and $z$.

### 2.2 Pasquill-Gifford Stability Classification

Atmospheric stability determines **how much the plume spreads** vertically and horizontally. **Pasquill (1961)** and later **Gifford (1961)** classified stability into six categories:

| Class | Stability Type | Meteorological Conditions | Plume Behavior |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| **A** | Very Unstable | Strong solar radiation, low wind | **Looping**, large vertical spread |
| **B** | Moderately Unstable | Moderate sun, moderate wind | Coning |
| **C** | Slightly Unstable | Light sun, moderate wind | Coning |
| **D** | Neutral | Overcast or strong wind, day or night | **Symmetric cone** (most common) |
| **E** | Slightly Stable | Clear night, light wind | Fanning |
| **F** | Moderately Stable | Clear night, calm | **Fumigation**, narrow vertical spread |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Classes **A–C** are **unstable** (daytime convection dominates); **D** is neutral; **E–F** are **stable** (nighttime inversions suppress vertical mixing).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ instead of $\vert$ in tables to avoid markdown breaking.

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | $C(x,y,z) = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right)\!\left[\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)+\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]$ | **Full Gaussian Plume Equation** (3-D steady-state) |
| 2 | $C(x,0,0) = \dfrac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$ | **Centerline ground-level** concentration ($y=0, z=0$) |
| 3 | $C(x,y,0) = \dfrac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right)\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$ | **Ground-level** concentration (any $y$) |
| 4 | $\sigma_y, \sigma_z = f(x, \text{stability class})$ | Plume spread functions (Pasquill-Gifford curves) |
| 5 | $\sigma_y^2 = \dfrac{1}{2}C_y^2\, x^{2-n}$ | **Sutton's** horizontal variance |
| 6 | $\sigma_z^2 = \dfrac{1}{2}C_z^2\, x^{2-n}$ | **Sutton's** vertical variance |
| 7 | $C_{\max} = \dfrac{2Q}{e\pi u H^2}\sqrt{\dfrac{S_a}{S_b}}$ | Max ground concentration under **wind speed variation** (where $S_a = \sigma_z/\sigma_y$ slope) |
| 8 | $C_{\max} = \dfrac{0.1171\, Q}{u\, \sigma_z\, \sigma_y}\Big\vert_{\max}$ | Approx. **max ground-level** concentration at stability D |
| 9 | $\Delta h = \dfrac{1.6\, F^{1/3}\, x_f^{2/3}}{u}$ (if $F<55$), $\Delta h = \dfrac{38.71\, F^{5/8}}{u}$ (if $F \geq 55$) | **Briggs plume rise** (Buoyancy flux $F$ in m⁴/s³) |
| 10 | $F = g\, V_s\, d_s^2\, \dfrac{(T_s - T_a)}{4T_s}$ | **Buoyancy flux** (g = 9.81 m/s²) |

### 2.4 Sutton's Diffusion Theory — Quick Notes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sutton (1932)** proposed diffusion parameters $C_y$ and $C_z$ (virtual diffusion coefficients, m$^{\,n/2}$) and a stability index $n$ ($0 < n < 1$).

**Typical Sutton parameters:**

| Stability | $n$ | $C_y$ (m$^{\,n/2}$) | $C_z$ (m$^{\,n/2}$) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Strong Lapse (A) | 0.20 | 0.40 | 0.41 |
| Moderate Lapse (B) | 0.25 | 0.36 | 0.37 |
| Slight Lapse (C) | 0.30 | 0.32 | 0.31 |
| Neutral (D) | 0.35 | 0.31 | 0.27 |
| Moderate Inversion (E) | 0.40 | 0.28 | 0.21 |
| Strong Inversion (F) | 0.50 | 0.24 | 0.15 |

**Sutton's Plume Equation** (ground-level, centerline):

$$C(x,0,0) = \frac{Q}{\pi\, C_y\, C_z\, u\, x^{2-n}}$$

**Real-world utility:** Gaussian & Sutton models underpin **EPA regulatory models** (e.g., **ISC-AERMOD**) used in environmental impact assessments of power plants, refineries, and industrial complexes worldwide.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Gaussian Plume Equation

We start from **Fick's Law of Diffusion** in the turbulent atmosphere. The 3-D advection-diffusion equation for a conservative pollutant is:

$$u \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = K_y \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial y^2} + K_z \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial z^2}$$

**Step 1 — Recognize the steady-state simplification.**
Time-derivative is zero (continuous emission). Wind advects plume along $x$ only. Fickian turbulent diffusion occurs in $y$ and $z$.

$$u \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = K_y \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial y^2} + K_z \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial z^2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Gaussian (Normal) assumption in $y$ and $z$.**
The solution to a 1-D diffusion equation with a point source has the form:

$$C(y, z) \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right)\exp\!\left(-\frac{z^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the elevated point source at height $H$.**
A point source at $(0, 0, H)$ releases $Q$ (g/s). The center of the Gaussian shifts from $z = 0$ to $z = H$.

$$C(x,y,z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z}\exp\!\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right)\exp\!\left(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Apply ground-reflection principle.**
The ground ($z = 0$) reflects the plume. We superimpose a fictitious *image source* at $z = -H$. By linearity of diffusion:

$$\boxed{\,C(x,y,z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z}\exp\!\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right)\!\left[\exp\!\left(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)+\exp\!\left(-\frac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]\,}$$

**Step 5 — Special case: ground-level concentration.**
Set $z = 0$:

$$C(x,y,0) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z}\exp\!\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right)\left[2\exp\!\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]$$

$$C(x,y,0) = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z}\exp\!\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right)\exp\!\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Centerline ground concentration (worst-case at fixed $x$).**
Set $y = 0$:

$$\boxed{\,C(x,0,0) = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z}\exp\!\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic Summary:** Each derivation step adds one physical reality — (a) turbulent diffusion → Gaussian profile; (b) elevated source → shift to $z = H$; (c) ground reflection → image source at $-H$; (d) ground-level evaluation → set $z=0$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Sutton's Diffusion Equations

**Step 1 — Starting point: empirical observations.**
Wind-tunnel and field data show that plume spread in turbulent flow follows a **power-law** with downwind distance $x$:

$$\sigma_y \propto x^{\alpha_y}, \qquad \sigma_z \propto x^{\alpha_z}$$

**Step 2 — Sutton's postulate.**
Define $C_y, C_z$ (virtual diffusion coefficients) and a stability parameter $n$ such that:

$$\sigma_y^2 = \frac{1}{2}C_y^2\, x^{2-n}$$

$$\sigma_z^2 = \frac{1}{2}C_z^2\, x^{2-n}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Gaussian plume equation.**
With $y = 0$, $z = 0$:

$$C(x,0,0) = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sqrt{\sigma_y^2 \sigma_z^2}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

$$C(x,0,0) = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}C_y^2\, x^{2-n} \cdot \frac{1}{2}C_z^2\, x^{2-n}}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{H^2}{C_z^2\, x^{2-n}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Simplify.**

$$\boxed{\,C(x,0,0) = \frac{2Q}{\pi\, C_y\, C_z\, u\, x^{2-n}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{2H^2}{C_z^2\, x^{2-n}}\right)\,}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
- $n \to 0$: highly unstable (large turbulence) → faster spread.
- $n \to 1$: highly stable (suppressed turbulence) → minimal spread.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A power plant emits SO₂ at $Q = 100$ g/s from an effective height $H = 80$ m. Wind speed $u = 4$ m/s. Stability class is **D** (neutral). Compute ground-level centerline concentration at $x = 2$ km.

**Given:** $Q = 100$ g/s; $H = 80$ m; $u = 4$ m/s; $x = 2000$ m; Class D.

**From Pasquill-Gifford curves (Class D, $x = 2000$ m):**
- $\sigma_y \approx 35$ m
- $\sigma_z \approx 22$ m

**Apply the centerline ground equation:**

$$C = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z}\exp\!\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

**Numerical substitution:**

$$C = \frac{100}{\pi \times 4 \times 35 \times 22}\exp\!\left(-\frac{80^2}{2\times 22^2}\right)$$

**Step-by-step:**
- Denominator: $\pi \times 4 \times 35 \times 22 = 9676.1$
- Pre-exponential: $100 / 9676.1 = 0.01034$ g/m³
- Exponent: $-\dfrac{6400}{2 \times 484} = -\dfrac{6400}{968} = -6.612$
- Exponential term: $e^{-6.612} = 0.00134$

$$C = 0.01034 \times 0.00134 = 1.39 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{g/m}^3 = 13.9\ \mu\text{g/m}^3$$

**Conversion to $\mu g/m^3$:** Multiply by $10^6$.
**Answer:** $C \approx 13.9\ \mu\text{g/m}^3$ (well within NAAQS for SO₂).

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Gaussian Plume — 3D Conceptual Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Point Source: Emission Rate Q g/s] --> B[Plume Rise Δh]
    B --> C[Effective Stack Height H = hs + Δh]
    C --> D[Wind Advection along x-axis with speed u]
    D --> E[Horizontal Spread σy with downwind distance x]
    D --> F[Vertical Spread σz with downwind distance x]
    E --> G[Gaussian Crosswind Profile in y-direction]
    F --> H[Gaussian Vertical Profile in z-direction]
    G --> I[Real Plume at z = H]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Ground Reflection via Image Source at z = -H]
    J --> K[Resultant Concentration Field C x y z]
    K --> L[Receptor at Ground z = 0 y = 0]
    L --> M[Centerline Ground Concentration Cmax]
```

### 4.2 Plume Behavior Under Different Stability Classes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Emission Source] --> B[Stability Class]
    B --> C1[Class A Very Unstable]
    B --> C2[Class B Moderately Unstable]
    B --> C3[Class D Neutral]
    B --> C4[Class F Very Stable]
    C1 --> D1[Looping Plume High Vertical Spread]
    C2 --> D2[Coning Plume Moderate Spread]
    C3 --> D3[Symmetric Cone Steady Spread]
    C4 --> D4[Fanning Plume Narrow Vertical Spread]
    D1 --> E[Maximum Downwind Dilution]
    D4 --> F[Minimum Downwind Dilution Hazardous Near Source]
```

### 4.3 Pasquill-Gifford Stability Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Determine Atmospheric Stability] --> Q1[Day or Night]
    Q1 --> Day[Daytime]
    Q1 --> Night[Nighttime]
    Day --> Q2[Incoming Solar Radiation Intensity]
    Q2 --> Strong[Strong Sun]
    Q2 --> Moderate[Moderate Sun]
    Q2 --> Slight[Slight Sun]
    Q2 --> Overcast[Overcast]
    Strong --> A[Class A Very Unstable]
    Moderate --> B[Class B Moderately Unstable]
    Slight --> C[Class C Slightly Unstable]
    Overcast --> D1[Class D Neutral]
    Night --> Q3[Wind Speed and Cloud Cover]
    Q3 --> ThinCloud[Thin Cloud or > 50 percent Cover]
    Q3 --> Clear[Clear Sky or Less Than 50 percent Cover]
    ThinCloud --> D2[Class D Neutral]
    Clear --> E[Class E Slightly Stable]
    Clear --> F[Class F Moderately Stable if Wind Less Than 2 m/s]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Dispersion Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input1[Meteorological Data: u direction T stability] --> Core
    Input2[Source Data: Q hs ds Ts] --> Core
    Input3[Receptor Data: x y z] --> Core
    Input4[Terrain and Building Inputs] --> Core
    Core[Gaussian Dispersion Engine] --> Process1[Compute Plume Rise Δh]
    Core --> Process2[Compute Effective Height H]
    Core --> Process3[Lookup Pasquill Gifford σy σz]
    Process1 --> Output
    Process2 --> Output
    Process3 --> Output
    Output[Concentration Field C x y z in microgram per cubic meter]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Note:** In the **Looping** case (Class A), hot thermals carry plume fragments up and down erratically — concentrations are highly variable. In **Fanning** (Class F), a temperature inversion caps vertical mixing — pollutants travel horizontally for tens of kilometers with little dilution, creating hazardous conditions near ground sources at the inversion break (fumigation).

**Define atmospheric stability. List the six Pasquill-Gifford stability classes with their typical meteorological conditions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Atmospheric stability refers to the atmosphere's ability to resist or enhance vertical motion. It governs the rate of vertical dispersion of pollutants.

**Six Stability Classes (2 marks):**
| Class | Type | Conditions |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| A | Very Unstable | Strong insolation, calm wind |
| B | Moderately Unstable | Moderate sun, moderate wind |
| C | Slightly Unstable | Slight sun, moderate wind |
| D | Neutral | Overcast, high wind, day/night |
| E | Slightly Stable | Clear night, light wind |
| F | Moderately Stable | Clear night, calm (< 2 m/s) |

**State the assumptions of the Gaussian Plume Model.**

2. **Uniform, steady wind** along $x$-direction with speed $u$. (0.5)

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice. Below are **two independent alternatives** as required by the KTU board pattern.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) Derive the Gaussian Plume Model equation starting from Fick's law of diffusion. List all assumptions clearly. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Steady-state, continuous point source emission rate $Q$.
2. Constant wind speed $u$ along $x$-axis.
3. Gaussian distribution of concentration in $y$ and $z$.
4. Conservative pollutant (no reaction/deposition).
5. Perfect ground reflection.
6. Homogeneous, flat terrain.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Starting from Fick's law for turbulent diffusion:

$$u \frac{\partial C}{\partial x} = K_y \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial y^2} + K_z \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial z^2}$$

Recognizing that turbulent eddies produce Gaussian spreading, the solution in the transverse ($y$) and vertical ($z$) directions has the form:

$$C \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right)\exp\!\left(-\frac{z^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

For a point source at height $H$ with emission rate $Q$, mass conservation gives the pre-factor $Q/(2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z)$:

$$C(x,y,z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z}\exp\!\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right)\exp\!\left(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

Applying ground reflection via image source at $z = -H$:

$$\boxed{C(x,y,z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z}\exp\!\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2}\right)\!\left[\exp\!\left(-\frac{(z-H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)+\exp\!\left(-\frac{(z+H)^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)\right]}$$

---

**(b) A 60 m tall stack emits SO₂ at 150 g/s. Wind speed at stack height is 5 m/s. Pasquill class is D. Estimate ground-level centerline concentration at 1 km downwind using Pasquill-Gifford curves (σy ≈ 25 m, σz ≈ 18 m). (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given (1 mark):**
- $Q = 150$ g/s, $H = 60$ m, $u = 5$ m/s, $x = 1000$ m
- $\sigma_y = 25$ m, $\sigma_z = 18$ m

**Formula (2 marks):**

$$C = \frac{Q}{\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z}\exp\!\left(-\frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$$

**Calculation (4 marks):**

Denominator:
$$\pi \times 5 \times 25 \times 18 = 7068.6$$

Pre-exponential:
$$\frac{150}{7068.6} = 0.02122\ \text{g/m}^3$$

Exponent:
$$-\frac{60^2}{2 \times 18^2} = -\frac{3600}{648} = -5.556$$

Exponential term:
$$e^{-5.556} = 0.00386$$

Final:
