$$C = 0.02122 \times 0.00386 = 8.19 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{g/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{C \approx 81.9\ \mu\text{g/m}^3}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) Explain Sutton's diffusion theory. State Sutton's equations for σy² and σz². Discuss how the stability parameter n affects dispersion. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Theory (3 marks):**
Sutton (1932) proposed empirical equations for the spread of a plume based on the observation that wind-turbulence data follow power-law profiles. He introduced virtual diffusion coefficients $C_y, C_z$ and a stability parameter $n$ that varies between 0 and 1 depending on atmospheric stability.

**Equations (2 marks):**

$$\sigma_y^2 = \frac{1}{2}C_y^2\, x^{2-n}$$

$$\sigma_z^2 = \frac{1}{2}C_z^2\, x^{2-n}$$

**Effect of $n$ (2 marks):**
- For **strong instability** (A): $n \approx 0.20$ → small $n$, large exponent $(2-n) \approx 1.8$ → plume spreads rapidly.
- For **neutral** (D): $n \approx 0.35$.
- For **strong stability** (F): $n \approx 0.50$ → large $n$, small exponent $(2-n) \approx 1.5$ → plume spread is suppressed.

---

**(b) An industrial chimney has effective height 100 m. Using Sutton's model with $C_y = 0.20$, $C_z = 0.12$, $n = 0.25$, $u = 5$ m/s, $Q = 200$ g/s, calculate the ground-level centerline concentration at $x = 500$ m. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given (1 mark):** $H = 100$ m, $C_y = 0.20$, $C_z = 0.12$, $n = 0.25$, $u = 5$ m/s, $Q = 200$ g/s, $x = 500$ m.

**Formula (2 marks):**

$$C(x,0,0) = \frac{2Q}{\pi\, C_y\, C_z\, u\, x^{2-n}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{2H^2}{C_z^2\, x^{2-n}}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Compute $x^{2-n}$:**
$$x^{2-n} = 500^{1.75} = 500^{1.75}$$

Using $500^{1.75} = 500^1 \times 500^{0.75} = 500 \times 105.7 = 52850$

**Step 2 — Compute pre-factor:**
$$\frac{2 \times 200}{\pi \times 0.20 \times 0.12 \times 5 \times 52850} = \frac{400}{19927} = 0.02008\ \text{g/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Compute exponent:**
$$-\frac{2 \times 100^2}{0.12^2 \times 52850} = -\frac{20000}{760.7} = -26.29$$

**Step 4 — Exponential term:**
$$e^{-26.29} \approx 3.87 \times 10^{-12}$$

**Final Answer (4 marks):**
$$C \approx 0.02008 \times 3.87 \times 10^{-12} \approx 7.77 \times 10^{-14}\ \text{g/m}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This extremely low concentration (essentially negligible) demonstrates Sutton's model prediction: at 500 m with strong stability and high stack, the plume has not yet descended to ground level — concentration buildup is minimal until downwind distance reaches the **critical distance $x_c$** where $\sigma_z \approx H/\sqrt{2}$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Dispersion Model Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the image-source term** (ground reflection) when writing the full plume equation. Lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Using $Q$ in wrong units** (kg/s vs g/s vs µg/s). Always check consistency with $C$ units.
> 3. **Incorrect assignment of Pasquill-Gifford stability class** from given conditions. Misreading "clear sky night, calm" as Class D instead of F costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Confusing $\sigma_y$ with $\sigma_z$** when substituting into the formula — read the PG curve carefully (horizontal vs vertical).
> 5. **Skipping the exponential term** when computing concentration. Many students stop at the pre-factor — **at least 50% of the concentration reduction comes from the exponential decay**.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions explicitly** in derivation questions — at least **1 mark** is reserved for listing assumptions.
> 7. **Forgetting that the Gaussian Plume Model is INVALID** under calm-wind (low $u$) or steep-terrain conditions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Atmospheric Diffusion Theories**

- ✅ **Gaussian Plume Model** is the cornerstone — derived from Fick's law under steady-state, Gaussian-spread assumptions.
- ✅ The **full 3-D plume equation** contains an **image-source term** for ground reflection; **ground-level centerline** is the most commonly tested special case.
- ✅ **$\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$** are functions of downwind distance $x$ and **Pasquill-Gifford stability class** (A–F).
- ✅ **Class A (unstable)** → looping, maximum vertical spread, fastest dilution.
- ✅ **Class F (stable)** → fanning, minimal vertical spread, hazardous fumigation potential.
- ✅ **Class D (neutral)** → symmetric cone, most commonly assumed in design.
- ✅ **Sutton's model** uses $C_y, C_z, n$ parameters; smaller $n$ → more unstable.
- ✅ **Briggs plume rise formulas** account for buoyancy (hot gases) and momentum; effective height $H = h_s + \Delta h$.
- ✅ **$C_{\max}$** (maximum ground concentration) occurs at critical $x_c$ where $dC/dx = 0$.
- ✅ **Gaussian Puff Model** (unsteady state) applies for short-duration releases; Plume Model assumes continuous release.
- ✅ **Validation criteria:** PG model is valid for $u \geq 1$ m/s; flat terrain; distances $0.1$ km to $10$ km.
- ✅ **Real-world models:** AERMOD, CALPUFF, ISCST3 are descendants of Gaussian/Sutton theory.
- ✅ **Numerical trap:** Always convert units — g/s with g/m³ gives g/m³; cross-check with NAAQS in µg/m³.
- ✅ **Remember the boundary condition:** $C \to 0$ as $x \to 0, y \to \pm\infty, z \to \pm\infty$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plume Rise - Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBT726 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Plume Rise - Numerical Problems # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Plume Rise ($\Delta h$)** is the vertical distance that a buoyant (or momentum-driven) stack plume travels **above the stack exit** before it becomes essentially level (i.e., before its vertical velocity becomes negligible compared with the horizontal wind velocity). It is a direct consequence of the **initial buoyancy flux** and **momentum flux** imparted to the flue gas at the stack mouth. The **Effective Stack Height (H)** — the height used in Gaussian plume dispersion modelling — is therefore: $$H = h_s + \Delta h$$ where: - $h_s$ = physical stack height (m) - $\Delta h$ = plume rise (m) > [!NOTE] > **Why is plume rise important in KTU?** Because in the Gaussian dispersion equation, ground-level concentration $C(x,y,0)$ is **inversely proportional to $H^2$**. Even a 10 m error in $\Delta h$ can change predicted concentrations by **20–40 %** — a frequent source of marks in KTU numerical problems. --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Hot-Air Balloon + A Rocket Booster" Imagine lighting a camp-fire: the smoke first shoots **upward** (rocket booster effect = **momentum**), then keeps rising because it is **hotter than surrounding air** (hot-air balloon effect = **buoyancy**). Finally, the wind blows it sideways and it flattens out — that flattening altitude above the chimney is the **plume rise**. | Real-world Effect | Engineering Term | |---|---| | Rocket booster puff at launch | **Momentum flux** | | Hot-air balloon floating up | **Buoyancy flux** | | Wind blowing balloon sideways | **Wind advection** | | Final levelling altitude above chimney | **Plume rise $\Delta h$** | --- ## 1.3 The Two Driving Fluxes > [!IMPORTANT] > Two **physical fluxes** drive plume rise. KTU examiners love to test which one dominates. **Buoyancy Flux ($F$)** — caused by hot gas being lighter than ambient air: $$F = g \, v_s \, \frac{d_s^{2}}{4} \, \frac{\Delta T}{T_s} \quad \text{(m}^4/\text{s}^3\text{)}$$ **Momentum Flux ($M$)** — caused by gas leaving the stack at high speed: $$M = \frac{v_s^{\,2} \, d_s^{\,2}}{4} \cdot \frac{T_a}{T_s} \quad \text{(m}^4/\text{s}^2\text{)}$$ Standard physical constants used throughout this module: - $g = \mathbf{9.81 \; m/s^{2}}$ (gravitational acceleration) - $R = \mathbf{8.314 \; J/(mol\cdot K)}$ (universal gas constant) - $P_{atm} \approx \mathbf{1013.25 \; mbar}$ at mean sea level --- ## 1.4 Atmospheric Stability & Plume Behaviour | Pasquill-Gifford Class | Stability | Plume Behaviour | |---|---|---| | A, B | Very Unstable / Unstable | **Looping** — high rise, large $\Delta h$ | | C, D | Slightly Unstable / Neutral | **Coning** — moderate, *use Briggs neutral formula* | | E, F | Stable / Very Stable | **Fanning/Fumigation** — *rise is suppressed* | > [!TIP] > In KTU numerical problems, unless explicitly stated otherwise, assume **neutral (D) stability** and use **Briggs' neutral/unstable formulas**. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Background — "Why does a plume rise?" When hot flue gas leaves a stack, two mechanisms act simultaneously: 1. **Initial momentum injection** — the vertical exit velocity $v_s$ gives the plume an upward "kick". This effect dominates for the first few stack diameters and decays as $v_s$ dissipates against air drag. 2. **Buoyant acceleration** — because the gas temperature $T_s$ exceeds ambient $T_a$, density difference creates an Archimedean upthrust. The plume accelerates until turbulence dilutes it to near-ambient temperature. Mathematically, plume rise depends on: - Buoyancy flux $F \propto v_s \, d_s^{2} \, \Delta T / T_s$ - **Wind speed $u$** in the denominator — a **stronger wind → lower plume rise** (the wind "bends the plume over" faster). --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Briggs + Holland) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table.** Every KTU plume-rise numerical is solved by picking the right row. | # | Formula | Applicable When | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $F = g \, v_s \, d_s^{2} \Delta T \, / \, (4 \, T_s)$ | Always — definition of buoyancy flux | m⁴/s³ | | 2 | $\Delta h = 21.3 \, F^{3/5} / u$ | $F < 55$ m⁴/s³ (neutral/unstable) | m | | 3 | $\Delta h = 38.7 \, F^{3/5} / u$ | $F \ge 55$ m⁴/s³ (neutral/unstable) | m | | 4 | $\Delta h = 1.6 \, F^{1/3} \, x_f^{2/3} / u$ | Distance-to-final-rise known | m | | 5 | $\Delta h = 3 d_s v_s / u$ | Momentum only; $v_s / u \le 1$ | m | | 6 | $\Delta h = 3 d_s (v_s/u)^{2}$ | Momentum only; $v_s / u > 1$ | m | | 7 | $\Delta h = \dfrac{v_s d_s}{u}\left(1.5 + 2.68 \times 10^{-3}\, P \, \dfrac{\Delta T}{T_s}\right)$ | **Holland's formula** (older, still asked) | m | | 8 | $H = h_s + \Delta h$ | Effective stack height (always) | m | > [!NOTE] > In Formula 7, $P$ is the **atmospheric pressure in millibars (mb)**. At sea level use $P \approx 1013$ mb. --- ## 2.3 Boundary / Threshold Conditions | Parameter | Threshold | Implication | |---|---|---| | $v_s / u$ | $\le 1$ | Plume rises **along** wind → use momentum formula (linear) | | $v_s / u$ | $> 1$ | Plume initially vertical → use momentum formula (quadratic) | | $F$ | $< 55$ m⁴/s³ | "**Small**" source — Briggs low-F formula | | $F$ | $\ge 55$ m⁴/s³ | "**Large**" source — Briggs high-F formula | | Stability | E or F | Add a stability correction; $\Delta h$ is reduced | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Power-plant stack design**: predicting plume rise determines minimum $h_s$ so that ground-level $\text{SO}_2$ / $\text{NO}_x$ stay within NAAQS. - **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)**: regulatory submissions for thermal power plants (e.g., NTPC Kerala projects) require plume-rise modelling under IS 5182 Part 12 protocols. - **Industrial stack height selection under EPA / CPCB norms**: $H$ appears directly in the GEP (Good Engineering Practice) stack-height formula. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Problems ## 3.1 Derivation — Buoyancy Flux $F$ from First Principles Consider a circular stack of exit diameter $d_s$, exit velocity $v_s$, exit temperature $T_s$, discharging into ambient air at $T_a$. Mass-flow rate of flue gas: $$\dot m = \rho_s \, A_s \, v_s = \rho_s \, \frac{\pi d_s^{2}}{4} \, v_s$$ Density (ideal gas): $\rho_s = P / (R T_s)$, $\rho_a = P / (R T_a)$. Buoyancy force per unit volume of plume element = $(\rho_a - \rho_s) \, g$. Substituting ideal-gas relation and writing $r_s = d_s/2$ : $$F_b = g \, v_s \, r_s^{2} \, \frac{T_s - T_a}{T_s}$$ $$\boxed{\,F = \frac{g \, v_s \, d_s^{\,2} \, (T_s - T_a)}{4 \, T_s}\,} \quad \text{m}^4/\text{s}^3$$ > **Why this is the master equation**: $F$ is the **only parameter** that enters Briggs' plume rise. Once you compute $F$, you are 80 % through the problem. --- ## 3.2 Worked Problem 1 — Briggs (Low-Buoyancy Case) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Question]** > A power-plant stack has the following characteristics: > $h_s = 60$ m, $d_s = 2$ m, $v_s = 10$ m/s, $T_s = 400$ K, $T_a = 300$ K, $u = 4$ m/s. > Calculate the **plume rise** and the **effective stack height** using Briggs' formula. Assume neutral stability. ### Step 1 — Identify driving quantities $$\Delta T = T_s - T_a = 400 - 300 = 100 \; \text{K}$$ $$\frac{\Delta T}{T_s} = \frac{100}{400} = 0.25$$ ### Step 2 — Compute buoyancy flux $F$ $$ \begin{aligned} F &= \frac{g \, v_s \, d_s^{\,2} \, \Delta T}{4 \, T_s} \\ &= \frac{9.81 \times 10 \times (2)^{2} \times 100}{4 \times 400} \\ &= \frac{9.81 \times 10 \times 4 \times 100}{1600} \\ &= \frac{39\,240}{1600} \\ &= 24.525 \; \text{m}^{4}/\text{s}^{3} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Choose the correct Briggs branch Since $F = 24.525 \; < \; 55 \; \text{m}^{4}/\text{s}^{3}$, we use the **low-F** formula: $$\Delta h = \frac{21.3 \, F^{3/5}}{u}$$ ### Step 4 — Evaluate $F^{3/5}$ $$ \begin{aligned} F^{3/5} &= (24.525)^{0.6} \\ \ln(24.525) &= 3.1998 \\ 0.6 \times 3.1998 &= 1.9199 \\ e^{1.9199} &= 6.821 \end{aligned} $$ $$\therefore \; F^{3/5} \approx 6.82$$ ### Step 5 — Compute plume rise $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta h &= \frac{21.3 \times 6.821}{4} \\ &= \frac{145.29}{4} \\ &\approx 36.32 \; \text{m} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 — Effective stack height $$ \begin{aligned} H &= h_s + \Delta h \\ &= 60 + 36.32 \\ &\approx 96.32 \; \text{m} \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > **Sanity check**: $\Delta h \approx 36$ m is reasonable — it is about 18× the stack diameter, which is typical for buoyant plumes from large stacks. --- ## 3.3 Worked Problem 2 — Briggs (High-Buoyancy Case) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Question]** > A 250 MW thermal plant stack: $h_s = 200$ m, $d_s = 5$ m, $v_s = 20$ m/s, $T_s = 450$ K, $T_a = 305$ K, $u = 6$ m/s. > Find the effective stack height. ### Step 1 — Buoyancy flux $$ \begin{aligned} F &= \frac{9.81 \times 20 \times 25 \times (450 - 305)}{4 \times 450} \\ &= \frac{9.81 \times 20 \times 25 \times 145}{1800} \\ &= \frac{711\,225}{1800} \\ &\approx 395.1 \; \text{m}^{4}/\text{s}^{3} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Branch selection $F = 395.1 \; > \; 55$, so use the **high-F formula**: $$\Delta h = \frac{38.7 \, F^{3/5}}{u}$$ ### Step 3 — Evaluate $F^{3/5}$ $$ \begin{aligned} F^{3/5} &= (395.1)^{0.6} \\ \ln(395.1) &= 5.979 \\ 0.6 \times 5.979 &= 3.587 \\ e^{3.587} &\approx 36.18 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Plume rise $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta h &= \frac{38.7 \times 36.18}{6} \\ &\approx \frac{1400.2}{6} \\ &\approx 233.4 \; \text{m} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Effective stack height $$H = 200 + 233.4 = 433.4 \; \text{m}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common mistake**: forgetting to **square** the diameter — many students write $F = g v_s d_s \Delta T / T_s$ and get an answer that is **4× too small**, losing 2 marks instantly. --- ## 3.4 Worked Problem 3 — Holland's Formula > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]** > For a stack with $d_s = 1.5$ m, $v_s = 12$ m/s, $T_s = 423$ K, $T_a = 303$ K, $u = 5$ m/s, $P = 1010$ mb, $h_s = 80$ m. Compute plume rise using **Holland's formula** and the effective stack height. ### Step 1 — Compute the geometric term $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{v_s d_s}{u} &= \frac{12 \times 1.5}{5} = \frac{18}{5} = 3.6 \; \text{m} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Compute the thermal correction factor $$ \begin{aligned} 2.68 \times 10^{-3} \, P \, \frac{\Delta T}{T_s} &= 2.68 \times 10^{-3} \times 1010 \times \frac{120}{423} \\ &= 2.707 \times 0.2837 \\ &\approx 0.768 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Apply Holland's formula $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta h &= 3.6 \times \left(1.5 + 0.768\right) \\ &= 3.6 \times 2.268 \\ &\approx 8.17 \; \text{m} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Effective stack height $$H = 80 + 8.17 = 88.17 \; \text{m}$$ > [!NOTE] > Holland's formula usually gives a **smaller** $\Delta h$ than Briggs — note the different physical stack diameters and exit velocities used here. Always read the question carefully for **which formula** is required. --- ## 3.5 Worked Problem 4 — Momentum-Dominated Plume > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]** > A small diesel exhaust stack: $d_s = 0.4$ m, $v_s = 25$ m/s, $u = 6$ m/s. Gas is at ambient temperature (no buoyancy, $\Delta T = 0$). Find $\Delta h$. ### Step 1 — Compute $v_s / u$ $$\frac{v_s}{u} = \frac{25}{6} \approx 4.17 \; > \; 1$$ ### Step 2 — Apply the appropriate momentum-rise formula $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta h &= 3 \, d_s \left(\frac{v_s}{u}\right)^{2} \\ &= 3 \times 0.4 \times (4.17)^{2} \\ &= 1.2 \times 17.39 \\ &\approx 20.86 \; \text{m} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.6 Worked Problem 5 — Comparison & Choice of Formula > **[Comprehensive KTU Practice Question]** > A chimney releases flue gas at $v_s = 15$ m/s from a stack of $d_s = 3$ m, $T_s = 380$ K, into $T_a = 290$ K. Wind speed $u = 5$ m/s, $h_s = 75$ m. Compute $\Delta h$ using **(a)** Briggs' formula and **(b)** Holland's formula ($P = 1013$ mb). Comment on the difference. ### Part (a) — Briggs $$ \begin{aligned} F &= \frac{9.81 \times 15 \times 9 \times 90}{4 \times 380} \\ &= \frac{119\,169}{1520} \\ &\approx 78.4 \; \text{m}^{4}/\text{s}^{3} \quad (>55, \text{so high-F branch}) \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} F^{3/5} &= (78.4)^{0.6} \\ \ln(78.4) &= 4.362 \\ 0.6 \times 4.362 &= 2.617 \\ e^{2.617} &\approx 13.69 \end{aligned} $$ $$\Delta h_{Briggs} = \frac{38.7 \times 13.69}{5} \approx 105.96 \; \text{m}$$ ### Part (b) — Holland $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{v_s d_s}{u} &= \frac{15 \times 3}{5} = 9 \; \text{m} \\ 2.68 \times 10^{-3} \times 1013 \times \frac{90}{380} &= 0.6435 \end{aligned} $$ $$\Delta h_{Holland} = 9 \times (1.5 + 0.6435) = 9 \times 2.1435 \approx 19.29 \; \text{m}$$ ### Comment Briggs gives a **much higher** plume rise because Holland's formula does not account for the **downwind distance** of final rise and uses a smaller buoyancy coefficient. For regulatory / EIA work in India, **Briggs is preferred**. --- ## 3.7 KTU Examiner's Master Decision Flowchart (Numerical) > **Question received → Follow these 4 steps:** > 1. Write down **all six given variables**: $h_s, d_s, v_s, T_s, T_a, u$ (and $P$ if Holland). > 2. Compute **buoyancy flux $F$** using Formula 1. > 3. If question says "**momentum only**" or $\Delta T = 0$ → use Formulas 5/6. Else → Briggs (Formula 2 or 3). > 4. Final answer: $\Delta h + h_s = H$. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Plume-Rise Schematic (Block Diagram) The diagram below represents the physical structure of plume rise, replacing a freehand sketch with a labelled functional block architecture (mermaid-compliant). ```mermaid flowchart TD A["STACK EXIT
height hs
diameter ds
velocity vs
temperature Ts"] B["BUOYANCY FLUX F
F = g vs ds2 DT 4 Ts
unit m4 per s3"] C["MOMENTUM FLUX M
M = vs2 ds2 Ta 4 Ts
unit m4 per s2"] D{"WHICH CASE
DT ZERO OR NOT"} E["MOMENTUM RISE
if vs u lt 1: dh = 3 ds vs per u
if vs u gt 1: dh = 3 ds vs u squared"] F["BRIGGS NEUTRAL RISE
F lt 55: dh = 21.3 F to 3 5 per u
F ge 55: dh = 38.7 F to 3 5 per u"] G["HOLLANDS FORMULA
dh = vs ds per u times 1.5 plus 2.68e-3 P DT Ts"] H["PLUME RISE DELTA H
metres"] I["EFFECTIVE STACK HEIGHT H
H = hs plus DELTA H
used in Gaussian plume"] A --> B A --> C B --> D C --> D D -- "yes delta T = 0" --> E D -- "no delta T nonzero" --> F D -- "Holland specified" --> G E --> H F --> H G --> H H --> I ``` ## 4.2 Plume-Rise Behaviour Under Different Stabilities ```mermaid flowchart LR A["ATMOSPHERIC
STABILITY CLASS"] B["A or B
UNSTABLE
LOOPING PLUME"] C["C or D
NEUTRAL
CONING PLUME"] D["E or F
STABLE
FANNING PLUME"] E["USE BRIGGS
NEUTRAL FORMULA"] F["USE BRIGGS
NEUTRAL FORMULA
HIGHEST RISE"] G["APPLY STABILITY
CORRECTION
LOWEST RISE"] A --> B A --> C A --> D B --> F C --> E D --> G ``` ## 4.3 Plume-Rise Calculation Workflow (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["STEP 1
List all given data
hs ds vs Ts Ta u"] S2["STEP 2
Compute buoyancy flux F"] S3["STEP 3
Compare F with 55
Choose Briggs branch"] S4["STEP 4
Compute F to power 3 5"] S5["STEP 5
Apply formula for DELTA H"] S6["STEP 6
Add hs to get effective H"] S7["STEP 7
Report DELTA H and H
with correct units"] S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q1. Define plume rise. Why is it important in Gaussian plume dispersion modelling?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 m):** Plume rise $\Delta h$ is the vertical distance the plume travels above the stack exit due to initial momentum and buoyancy before becoming horizontal. - **Significance (1 m):** It increases the effective stack height $H = h_s + \Delta h$, which decreases predicted ground-level concentrations since $C \propto 1/H^{2}$. - **Numerical implication (1 m):** Even a 10 % error in $\Delta h$ may lead to ~20 % error in ground-level concentration estimates; hence plume-rise modelling is critical for EIA and regulatory compliance. > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Q2. Define buoyancy flux $F$ and state its SI units. Explain why wind speed appears in the **denominator** of plume-rise formulas.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 m):** $F = g v_s d_s^{2} \Delta T / (4 T_s)$, units **m⁴/s³**. - **Physics (2 m):** Wind speed $u$ advects the plume horizontally; the **faster** the wind, the **less time** the plume has to rise vertically before being carried downwind. Therefore, stronger winds → lower plume rise, hence $u$ in the denominator. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - Forgetting to **square** $d_s$ (i.e. using $d_s$ instead of $d_s^{2}$) — loses **2 marks**. > - Using $T_a$ in the denominator instead of $T_s$ — loses **1 mark**. > - Not checking whether $F < 55$ or $\ge 55$ before applying Briggs — loses **1 mark**. > - Reporting plume rise without units (m) — loses **½ mark**. > - Mixing up Holland and Briggs formulas when the question specifically asks for one — loses **2–3 marks**. --- ### **Question A — 14 Marks** *(Comprehensive Briggs-type problem)* > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]** > A 500 MW thermal power plant stack has the following parameters: > $h_s = 220$ m, $d_s = 6$ m, $v_s = 25$ m/s, $T_s = 460$ K, $T_a = 308$ K, $u = 8$ m/s, atmospheric pressure $P = 1010$ mb. > > **(a)** Compute the **buoyancy flux** and the **plume rise** using Briggs' formula for neutral stability. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** If the wind speed drops to $u = 4$ m/s, recalculate the plume rise. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution ### Part (a) — 7 marks **Step 1 — Identify driving quantities (1 m)** $\Delta T = 460 - 308 = 152$ K, $T_s = 460$ K, $\Delta T / T_s = 152/460 = 0.3304$. **Step 2 — Buoyancy flux (3 m)** $$ \begin{aligned} F &= \frac{g \, v_s \, d_s^{2} \, \Delta T}{4 \, T_s} \\ &= \frac{9.81 \times 25 \times 36 \times 152}{4 \times 460} \\ &= \frac{1\,341\,996}{1840} \\ &\approx 729.35 \; \text{m}^{4}/\text{s}^{3} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Briggs branch selection (1 m)** Since $F = 729.35 \;\text{m}^{4}/\text{s}^{3} \;>\; 55$, we use the **high-F branch**: $$\Delta h = \frac{38.7 \, F^{3/5}}{u}$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate $F^{3/5}$ (1 m)** $$ \begin{aligned} F^{3/5} &= (729.35)^{0.6} \\ \ln(729.35) &= 6.592 \\ 0.6 \times 6.592 &= 3.955 \\ e^{3.955} &\approx 52.30 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Plume rise (1 m)** $$ \Delta h = \frac{38.7 \times 52.30}{8} \approx 252.98 \; \text{m} $$ ### Part (b) — 7 marks (Wind speed drops to 4 m/s) **Step 1 — Re-evaluate $\Delta h$ (3 m)** $F$ does **not change** (only depends on stack gas parameters, not $u$): $$\Delta h_{new} = \frac{38.7 \times 52.30}{4} \approx 505.96 \; \text{m}$$ **Step 2 — Effective stack heights (2 m)** $$H_{old} = 220 + 253 = 473 \; \text{m}, \qquad H_{new} = 220 + 506 = 726 \; \text{m}$$ **Step 3 — Interpretation (2 m)** A drop in wind speed from 8 → 4 m/s **doubles** the plume rise, because the plume now has more time to rise before being bent over. This is why pollutant concentrations near tall stacks can spike during low-wind, stable conditions. --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Holland + Briggs comparison problem)* > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]** > A cement plant stack has $h_s = 100$ m, $d_s = 4$ m, $v_s = 18$ m/s, $T_s = 430$ K, $T_a = 305$ K, $u = 6$ m/s, $P = 1005$ mb. > > **(a)** Calculate the plume rise using **Holland's formula**. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Calculate the plume rise using **Briggs' formula** (neutral stability) and comment on which estimate is more appropriate for regulatory use. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution ### Part (a) — Holland (7 marks) **Step 1 — Geometric term (2 m)** $$ \frac{v_s d_s}{u} = \frac{18 \times 4}{6} = 12 \; \text{m} $$ **Step 2 — Thermal factor (3 m)** $$ \begin{aligned} 2.68 \times 10^{-3} \times P \times \frac{\Delta T}{T_s} &= 2.68 \times 10^{-3} \times 1005 \times \frac{125}{430} \\ &= 2.6934 \times 0.2907 \\ &\approx 0.783 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Plume rise (2 m)** $$ \Delta h = 12 \times (1.5 + 0.783) = 12 \times 2.283 \approx 27.4 \; \text{m} $$ $$H_{Holland} = 100 + 27.4 = 127.4 \; \text{m}$$ ### Part (b) — Briggs (7 marks) **Step 1 — Buoyancy flux (2 m)** $$ \begin{aligned} F &= \frac{9.81 \times 18 \times 16 \times 125}{4 \times 430} \\ &= \frac{353\,160}{1720} \\ &\approx 205.3 \; \text{m}^{4}/\text{s}^{3} \quad (>55 \Rightarrow \text{high-F branch}) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — $F^{3/5}$ (2 m)** $$ \begin{aligned} F^{3/5} &= (205.3)^{0.6} \\ \ln(205.3) &= 5.324 \\ 0.6 \times 5.324 &= 3.194 \\ e^{3.194} &\approx 24.40 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Plume rise (2 m)** $$ \Delta h_{Briggs} = \frac{38.7 \times 24.40}{6} \approx 157.4 \; \text{m} $$ $$H_{Briggs} = 100 + 157.4 = 257.4 \; \text{m}$$ **Step 4 — Comment (1 m)** Briggs' formula gives a much higher $\Delta h$ because it accounts for the **final rise distance** ($x_f \approx 10 \, h_s$ for buoyant plumes) and the more physically realistic 3/5 power dependence. **Briggs is recommended** for modern regulatory work; Holland tends to **underestimate** plume rise for large hot stacks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-revision checklist for KTU exam day:** - ✅ **Plume rise** $\Delta h$ = height plume rises above stack due to **buoyancy + momentum**. - ✅ **Effective stack height** $H = h_s + \Delta h$ — always in metres. - ✅ **Buoyancy flux** $F = g v_s d_s^{2} \Delta T / (4 T_s)$ — units **m⁴/s³**. - ✅ **Briggs — low F** ($F < 55$): $\Delta h = 21.3 \, F^{3/5} / u$. - ✅ **Briggs — high F** ($F \ge 55$): $\Delta h = 38.7 \, F^{3/5} / u$. - ✅ **Holland**: $\Delta h = \dfrac{v_s d_s}{u}\left(1.5 + 2.68\times 10^{-3} \, P \, \Delta T / T_s\right)$, $P$ in mb. - ✅ **Momentum only** ($\Delta T = 0$): $\Delta h = 3 d_s v_s / u$ if $v_s/u \le 1$; $\Delta h = 3 d_s (v_s/u)^{2}$ if $v_s/u > 1$. - ✅ **Wind speed $u$ is in the denominator** — higher wind ⇒ lower plume rise. - ✅ **Default stability** is **neutral (D)** unless the question says otherwise. - ✅ **Units of $F$ are m⁴/s³**, not m³/s³ — this is a frequent KTU trap. - ✅ Briggs **always overestimates** $\Delta h$ relative to Holland for tall, hot stacks — remember this when comparing. - ✅ Memorise $g = 9.81$ m/s²; $P_{atm} \approx 1013$ mb at sea level. - ✅ Final answer **must include units** (m) and the **effective stack height** $H$.

topics.title: Gas Particle Interaction - Working Principle, Design, and Performance Equations of Gravity Separators, Centrifugal Separators, Fabric Filters, Particulate Scrubbers, Electrostatic Precipitators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBT726 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Gas Particle Interaction - Working Principle, Design, and Performance Equations of Gravity Separators, Centrifugal Separators, Fabric Filters, Particulate Scrubbers, Electrostatic Precipitators # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Gas-Particle Interaction — Foundational Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition — Gas Particle Interaction** > Gas-particle interaction refers to the physical phenomena governing the behavior of fine particulates (aerosols) suspended in a gas stream, including inertia, drag force, gravitational settling, diffusion, electrical charging, and Brownian motion. These interactions dictate the design and performance of all mechanical and electrostatic air pollution control devices. **Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate):** | Term | Engineering Definition | Standard Unit | |---|---|---| | Aerodynamic Diameter ($d_p$) | Diameter of a unit density sphere having the same settling velocity as the particle | m (micrometers used) | | Slip Correction Factor ($C_c$) | Correction factor applied to Stokes' law for particles comparable in size to mean free path | Dimensionless | | Mean Free Path ($\lambda$) | Average distance traveled by gas molecules between collisions | **0.066 μm** (at NTP) | | Particle Reynolds Number ($Re_p$) | Dimensionless number characterizing flow regime around a particle | Dimensionless | | Drag Coefficient ($C_D$) | Function describing fluid resistance on a particle | Dimensionless | ## 1.2 The Cunningham Slip Correction Factor For sub-micron particles, gas can no longer be treated as a continuous fluid because the particle size approaches the **mean free path of gas molecules ($\lambda \approx 0.066 \text{ μm at } 25°C, 1 atm$)**. The Cunningham correction factor is: $$C_c = 1 + \frac{2\lambda}{d_p}\left(A_1 + A_2 \, e^{-A_3 d_p / 2\lambda}\right)$$ Standard empirical values: $A_1 = 1.142$, $A_2 = 0.558$, $A_3 = 0.999$. > [!NOTE] > **Why Slip Matters:** Without $C_c$, the calculated settling velocity for sub-micron particles (e.g., PM2.5) would be **drastically underestimated** — by up to 10x for $d_p = 0.1 \text{ μm}$. This is the single most common error in KTU numerical problems. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Understanding > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Pebble in Honey" Concept** > Imagine dropping different sized pebbles into honey: > - **Large pebbles** fall straight down quickly — gravity dominates. (Like dust > 50 μm in a settling chamber). > - **Tiny grains** are suspended and diffuse randomly — they collide with the "honey molecules" (gas molecules) and zig-zag (Brownian motion). This is why fabric filters must use diffusion mechanisms (diameter 0.1–2 μm). > - **Electrically charged grains** can be pulled sideways by an external field — like a magnet attracting iron filings. This is the principle of ESPs. > - **Spinning grains** thrown outward by centrifugal force — like a salad spinner drying lettuce leaves (cyclone separator). ## 1.4 Particle Behavior Map (Critical for Design Selection) | Particle Size Range | Dominant Mechanism | Best Suited Control Device | |---|---|---| | > 50 μm | Gravitational settling | Gravity Separators / Cyclones | | 5–50 μm | Inertial impaction | Cyclones, Scrubbers | | 0.5–5 μm | Diffusion + Inertial | Fabric Filters (Baghouses) | | < 0.5 μm | Diffusion, Electrostatic | ESPs, HEPA Filters | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Aerodynamic Drag and Settling Regimes The drag force on a spherical particle in a gas stream is given by: $$F_D = C_D \cdot \frac{\pi d_p^2}{4} \cdot \frac{\rho_g v^2}{2}$$ Where $C_D$ is the drag coefficient which depends on the particle Reynolds number $Re_p = \frac{d_p \, \rho_g \, v}{\mu}$. **Three Settling Regimes:** 1. **Stokes' Regime** ($Re_p < 1$): $C_D = \frac{24}{Re_p}$ → Smooth, streamlined flow. 2. **Transition Regime** ($1 < Re_p < 1000$): Empirical curves (e.g., Schiller-Naumann correlation $C_D = \frac{24}{Re_p}(1 + 0.15 Re_p^{0.687})$). 3. **Newton's Regime** ($Re_p > 1000$): $C_D \approx 0.44$ → Turbulent wake. ## 2.2 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET > [!IMPORTANT] > **EXAM GOLD — Memorize These Equations. They Appear in Nearly Every KTU Part B Question on Module 3.** | # | Equation | Formula | Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Terminal Settling Velocity (Stokes' Law) | $V_t = \dfrac{g d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_g) C_c}{18 \mu}$ | Gravity Separators | | 2 | Particle Reynolds Number | $Re_p = \dfrac{d_p \, v \, \rho_g}{\mu}$ | All Devices | | 3 | Stopping Distance | $S = \dfrac{\rho_p \, d_p^2 \, v}{18 \mu} C_c$ | Inertial Separators | | 4 | Cyclone Cut Diameter | $d_{pc} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \mu W}{\pi N_t V_s (\rho_p - \rho_g)}}$ | Cyclone Design | | 5 | Cyclone Pressure Drop | $\Delta P = \xi \dfrac{\rho_g V_s^2}{2}$ | Cyclone Design | | 6 | Cyclone Efficiency (Lapple) | $\eta = \dfrac{1}{1 + (d_{pc}/d_p)^2}$ | Cyclone Performance | | 7 | Deutsch-Anderson ESP Equation | $\eta = 1 - e^{-A\omega/Q}$ | ESP Performance | | 8 | Particle Migration Velocity (ESP) | $\omega = \dfrac{q_p E_C}{3 \pi \mu d_p}$ | ESP Design | | 9 | Scrubber Cut Diameter (Calvert) | $d_{50} = \sqrt{\dfrac{9 \mu d_d}{C \, \rho_p \, V_g} \cdot \left(\dfrac{Q_l}{Q_g}\right)}$ | Scrubber Design | | 10 | Fabric Filter Single-Fiber Efficiency | $\eta_f = \eta_D + \eta_R + \eta_I + \eta_G$ | Baghouse Design | | 11 | Filter Drag (Clean) | $\Delta P = \dfrac{f \mu v L}{d_f^2}$ | Baghouse Design | | 12 | Filter Drag (with Cake) | $\Delta P = K_c \cdot W$ | Baghouse Design | | 13 | Corona Current (Pee's Law) | $i = \dfrac{4 \pi \epsilon_0 k}{9} \cdot \left(\dfrac{2 j}{k \epsilon_0}\right)^{1/2} \cdot V(V - V_0)$ | ESP Electrical Design | > **Notation Key:** $\mu$ = gas viscosity (kg/m·s); $\rho_p, \rho_g$ = particle & gas density (kg/m³); $V_s$ = inlet velocity; $W$ = cyclone inlet width; $N_t$ = effective turns; $A$ = collection area; $Q$ = gas flow; $\omega$ = migration velocity; $f$ = fiber friction factor; $K_c$ = cake specific resistance. ## 2.3 Stopping Distance — The Key to Inertial Separation When a gas stream changes direction abruptly, particles continue in their original direction due to **inertia**. The distance traveled before stopping is: $$S = \frac{\rho_p d_p^2 v_0 C_c}{18 \mu}$$ **Significance:** A particle is captured if its stopping distance exceeds the geometric gap of the obstacle (cyclone, scrubber baffle, fabric fiber). ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > These principles are used in: > - **Cement Industry:** Cyclones for kiln dust recovery. > - **Thermal Power Plants:** ESPs for fly ash collection (99.9% efficiency mandated by CPCB). > - **Pharmaceuticals:** HEPA filters (extension of fabric filtration) for cleanrooms. > - **Steel Plants:** Scrubbers for sinter plant emissions. > - **Wood Industry:** Baghouses for sawdust collection. > - **Mining:** Gravity separators for ore dust pre-cleaning. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Terminal Settling Velocity (Stokes' Law) — Gravity Separators ### Step 1: Free Body Diagram of a Falling Particle A particle falling under gravity in a gas experiences three forces: - Gravitational Force (downward) - Buoyant Force (upward) - Drag Force (opposite to motion, upward when settling) ### Step 2: Force Balance at Terminal Velocity At **terminal velocity**, acceleration = 0, so net force = 0. $$F_{gravity} = F_{buoyancy} + F_{drag}$$ $$\frac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \rho_p g = \frac{\pi d_p^3}{6} \rho_g g + C_D \cdot \frac{\pi d_p^2}{4} \cdot \frac{\rho_g V_t^2}{2}$$ ### Step 3: Simplification The left side minus the buoyancy term: $$\frac{\pi d_p^3}{6} (\rho_p - \rho_g) g = C_D \cdot \frac{\pi d_p^2}{4} \cdot \frac{\rho_g V_t^2}{2}$$ Dividing both sides by $\frac{\pi d_p^2}{4}$: $$\frac{2 d_p (\rho_p - \rho_g) g}{3} = C_D \cdot \frac{\rho_g V_t^2}{2}$$ ### Step 4: Apply Stokes' Law Condition For $Re_p < 1$ (laminar regime), $C_D = \frac{24}{Re_p} = \frac{24 \mu}{d_p \rho_g V_t}$. Substituting: $$\frac{2 d_p (\rho_p - \rho_g) g}{3} = \frac{24 \mu}{d_p \rho_g V_t} \cdot \frac{\rho_g V_t^2}{2} = \frac{12 \mu V_t}{d_p}$$ ### Step 5: Final Terminal Velocity Expression Solving for $V_t$: $$V_t = \frac{g d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_g)}{18 \mu}$$ With Cunningham correction for fine particles: $$\boxed{V_t = \frac{g d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_g) C_c}{18 \mu}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why $V_t \propto d_p^2$:** A 2× increase in diameter produces a 4× increase in settling velocity. This is why settling chambers can remove only coarse particles economically. ## 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Cyclone Cut Diameter — Centrifugal Separators ### Step 1: Concept of Cut Diameter The **cut diameter ($d_{pc}$)** is the diameter of a particle collected with 50% efficiency. It defines the sharpness of cyclone separation. ### Step 2: Particle Settling in Centrifugal Field Inside a cyclone, the centrifugal acceleration is $a_c = \frac{V_t^2}{r}$, where $V_t$ is the tangential (inlet) velocity and $r$ is the radial distance. ### Step 3: Terminal Radial Velocity (analogous to Stokes') Replacing $g$ in the Stokes' equation with $V_t^2/r$: $$v_r = \frac{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_g) V_t^2}{18 \mu r} C_c$$ ### Step 4: Time Available for Separation A particle travels a radial distance $r$ (from outer radius to wall) in time: $$t = \int_{r_2}^{r_1} \frac{dr}{v_r} = \frac{18 \mu}{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_g) V_t^2 C_c} \int_{r_2}^{r_1} r \, dr = \frac{9 \mu (r_1^2 - r_2^2)}{d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_g) V_t^2 C_c}$$ ### Step 5: Equating with Gas Residence Time The gas makes $N_t$ turns through a path of effective length $L$ at velocity $V_s$: $$t = \frac{L}{V_s} = \frac{\pi N_t (r_1 + r_2)}{V_s}$$ For a **50% collection**, $t_{residence} = t_{particle}$, and setting $r_1 \approx r_2 \approx r$ and $L = 2\pi r N_t$: $$d_{pc} = \sqrt{\frac{9 \mu W}{2 \pi N_t V_s (\rho_p - \rho_g)}}$$ Where $W$ is the cyclone inlet width. ## 3.3 DERIVATION 3: Deutsch-Anderson Equation — Electrostatic Precipitator ### Step 1: Define Migration Velocity The particle's velocity toward the collection electrode in a uniform electric field $E_c$ is governed by force balance: $$q_p E_c = 3 \pi \mu d_p \omega \quad \text{(Stokes drag)}$$ This gives the **migration velocity**: $$\omega = \frac{q_p E_c}{3 \pi \mu d_p}$$ ### Step 2: Mass Balance on a Differential Element For a control volume of cross-section $A$ and thickness $dx$: $$\text{Rate of particles entering} - \text{Rate leaving} = \text{Rate collected}$$ $$-V \cdot dC = C \cdot \frac{A}{Q} \omega \, dx$$ Where $V$ is volume and $C$ is particle concentration. ### Step 3: Integration Across the ESP Length $L$ Rearranging and integrating from inlet ($C = C_0$ at $x=0$) to outlet ($C = C_L$ at $x=L$): $$\int_{C_0}^{C_L} \frac{dC}{C} = -\frac{A \omega}{Q} \int_0^L dx$$ ### Step 4: Exponential Decay Solution $$\ln\left(\frac{C_L}{C_0}\right) = -\frac{A \omega L}{Q} = -\frac{A \omega}{Q}$$ (Here $A \cdot L$ = total collection area) ### Step 5: Final Collection Efficiency $$\boxed{\eta = 1 - \frac{C_L}{C_0} = 1 - e^{-A\omega/Q}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Deutsch-Anderson equation assumes:** (1) Uniform field, (2) Perfect mixing (worst-case), (3) Negligible re-entrainment, (4) Constant $\omega$. Real ESPs show 5-15% lower efficiency than this predicts. ## 3.4 DERIVATION 4: Scrubber Cut Diameter (Calvert's Model) ### Step 1: Concept Particles are collected by inertial impaction on liquid droplets of diameter $d_d$. The inertial impaction parameter $P$ (Stokes number) is: $$P = \frac{\rho_p d_p^2 v}{18 \mu d_d} = \frac{S}{d_d}$$ ### Step 2: Empirical Efficiency Correlation Calvert (1972) proposed a correlation for a well-designed scrubber: $$\eta(d_p) = \left(\frac{P}{P + a}\right)^2$$ Where $a$ is a constant related to scrubber geometry (typically 0.3–0.7 for venturi scrubbers). ### Step 3: Solving for Cut Diameter ($d_{50}$) Setting $\eta = 0.5$: $$0.5 = \left(\frac{P_{50}}{P_{50} + a}\right)^2 \implies P_{50} = a(1 + \sqrt{2})$$ ### Step 4: Solve for $d_{50}$ in Terms of Droplet Size and Slip Velocity For a venturi scrubber with slip velocity $V_{slip}$: $$d_{50} = \sqrt{\frac{18 \mu d_d \, a(1 + \sqrt{2})}{\rho_p V_{slip} C_c}}$$ Where $V_{slip}$ depends on gas-to-liquid flow ratio: $$V_{slip} \approx \frac{V_g}{1 + (Q_l/Q_g)\rho_g/\rho_l}$$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Gas-Particle Interaction System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB Inlet[Polluted Gas Inlet
PM Coarse Fine Ultrafine] --> AERODYN[Aerodynamic Forces
Drag Gravity Inertia] Inlet --> THERMAL[Thermal Effects
Brownian Motion Diffusion] Inlet --> ELEC[Electrical Effects
Charging Coulomb Force] Inlet --> OPTICAL[Optical Effects
Light Scattering] AERODYN --> GR[Gravity Separator
Stokes Settling] AERODYN --> CY[Centrifugal Cyclone
Inertial Separation] THERMAL --> FB[Fabric Filter Baghouse
Diffusion Impaction] THERMAL --> SC[Particulate Scrubber
Wet Collection] ELEC --> ESP[Electrostatic Precipitator
Charged Collection] GR --> GROUT[Clean Gas Stack] CY --> CYOUT[Clean Gas Stack] FB --> FBOUT[Clean Gas Stack] SC --> SCOUT[Clean Gas Stack Demister] ESP --> ESPOUT[Clean Gas Stack] GROUT --> KPI1[Collection Efficiency 40 to 70 percent] CYOUT --> KPI2[Collection Efficiency 70 to 95 percent] FBOUT --> KPI3[Collection Efficiency 99 to 99.9 percent] SCOUT --> KPI4[Collection Efficiency 90 to 99 percent] ESPOUT --> KPI5[Collection Efficiency 99 to 99.9 percent] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Cyclone Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR GasIn[Tangential Gas Inlet] --> Vortex[Outer Vortex Descending] Vortex --> Cone[Cone Section
Vmax at Wall] Cone --> Vortex2[Inner Vortex Ascending] Vortex2 --> VortexPipe[Vortex Finder] VortexPipe --> GasOut[Clean Gas Outlet] Vortex --> Dust[Dust Layer at Wall] Dust --> Hopper[Collection Hopper] Hopper --> DustOut[Dust Discharge] Inertia[Particle Inertia] -.->|Hitting Wall| Dust ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — ESP Working Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB HV[High Voltage DC Source
30 to 100 kV] --> Rect[Rectifier Transformer] Rect --> Wire[Discharge Electrode Wires] Wire --> Corona[Corona Discharge
Negative Ions] GasIn[Dirty Gas Inlet] --> Plenum1[Inlet Plenum
Diffuser Baffles] Plenum1 --> Field[Collection Field
Parallel Plates] Corona --> Field Field --> Plates[Collection Plates Positive] Plates --> Cake[Dust Layer on Plates] Cake --> Rapping[Rapper System
Mechanical Vibration] Rapping --> Hopper2[Hopper] Field --> Plenum2[Outlet Plenum] Plenum2 --> GasOut[Clean Gas Outlet] Hopper2 --> DustOut2[Collected Particulate Outlet] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Fabric Filter Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR FilterA[Filter Compartment A
In Filtration Mode] --> DP1[Pressure Drop Rising] DP1 --> Limit[Reaches Setpoint
e.g. 150 mm WC] Limit --> Isol[Inlet Damper Closed] Isol --> Vent[Vent to Atmosphere] Vent --> CleanPulse[Reverse Pulse Jet Air] CleanPulse --> Cake[Filter Cake Detached] Cake --> Hopper3[Hopper] Cake --> Reuse[Filter Bag Restored] Reuse --> FilterA ``` ## 4.5 Venturi Scrubber Schematic Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR GasA[Hot Dust Laden Gas] --> Throat[Venturi Throat
High Velocity 60 to 180 m/s] LiquidA[Liquid Spray In] --> SprayA[Atomizing Nozzle] SprayA --> Throat Throat --> Impaction[Inertial Impaction
Droplet Capture] Impaction --> Cyclonic[Entrainment Separator] Cyclonic --> CleanGas[Clean Saturated Gas] Cyclonic --> Slurry[Slurry Outlet to Treatment] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. State Stokes' Law. What is its significance in the design of gravity separators for air pollution control? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** Stokes' Law states that the terminal settling velocity of a spherical particle in a laminar flow regime is given by: $$V_t = \frac{g d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_g) C_c}{18 \mu}$$ **Significance in Gravity Separators:** - It determines the **minimum residence time** required for a particle of diameter $d_p$ to settle to the bottom of the chamber. - The chamber length $L$ must satisfy $L \leq V_g \cdot t_{settling}$, where $t_{settling} = H/V_t$ and $H$ is the chamber height. - Predicts that fine particles (< 50 μm) settle extremely slowly, making gravity separators inefficient for sub-micron dust. - The $d_p^2$ dependence shows that separator size must be very large to capture fine particles, hence it is used as a **pre-cleaner** upstream of more efficient devices. --- ### Q2. Differentiate between primary and secondary dust collection mechanisms in a fabric filter. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Primary Collection (Clean Filter) | Secondary Collection (With Dust Cake) | |---|---|---| | **Mechanism** | Diffusion + Inertial Impaction + Interception | Sieving and depth filtration by cake | | **Particle Size Efficiency** | High for <1 μm and >5 μm | High across all sizes after cake forms | | **Pressure Drop** | Low initially | Increases linearly with cake weight | | **Duration** | First few minutes | Dominant in steady operation | | **Fiber Properties** | Critical | Less critical once cake is established | The cake formed by primary collection actually becomes the **real filter medium** during steady operation. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (Option 1):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` **(a) A cyclone separator is to be designed to handle 10,000 m³/h of flue gas at 150°C with a particle density of 2000 kg/m³. The gas viscosity is 2.5 × 10⁻⁵ kg/m·s and density 0.85 kg/m³. If the inlet velocity is 15 m/s, the effective number of turns is 5, and the cyclone width is 0.3 m, determine the cut diameter. State any assumptions. [7 Marks]** **(b) With a neat diagram, explain the working principle of an electrostatic precipitator. State the Deutsch-Anderson equation and discuss the factors affecting the efficiency of an ESP. [7 Marks]** --- #### **Solution (a):** **Given:** - $Q = 10{,}000 \, m^3/h = 2.778 \, m^3/s$ - $T = 150°C = 423 \, K$ - $\rho_p = 2000 \, kg/m^3$ - $\mu = 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \, kg/(m \cdot s)$ - $\rho_g = 0.85 \, kg/m^3$ - $V_s = 15 \, m/s$ - $N_t = 5$ turns - $W = 0.3 \, m$ **Step 1: Write the cut-diameter formula** $$d_{pc} = \sqrt{\frac{9 \mu W}{2 \pi N_t V_s (\rho_p - \rho_g)}}$$ **Step 2: Substitute values** $$d_{pc} = \sqrt{\frac{9 \times (2.5 \times 10^{-5}) \times 0.3}{2 \pi \times 5 \times 15 \times (2000 - 0.85)}}$$ **Step 3: Calculate numerator** $$\text{Numerator} = 9 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.3 = 6.75 \times 10^{-5}$$ **Step 4: Calculate denominator** $$\text{Denominator} = 2 \pi \times 5 \times 15 \times 1999.15 = 942{,}477.8$$ **Step 5: Compute the ratio** $$\frac{6.75 \times 10^{-5}}{942{,}477.8} = 7.16 \times 10^{-11}$$ **Step 6: Take square root** $$d_{pc} = \sqrt{7.16 \times 10^{-11}} = 8.46 \times 10^{-6} \, m = 8.46 \, \mu m$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Particles of diameter 8.46 μm are collected with **50% efficiency**. Particles larger than this are removed with progressively higher efficiency, while smaller particles are removed with lower efficiency. **Assumptions:** - Stokes' regime flow (laminar flow around particle). - Uniform particle density and spherical shape. - Isothermal, steady-state flow. - Negligible re-entrainment from cyclone wall. - The slip correction factor $C_c \approx 1$ (since $d_{pc} >> \lambda$). --- #### **Solution (b):** **Working Principle of ESP:** > [!NOTE] > **ESP Operation (3-Stage Process):** > 1. **Charging Stage:** A high negative DC voltage (30–100 kV) applied to discharge electrodes (thin wires) creates a **corona discharge** producing negative gas ions. > 2. **Collection Stage:** The ions attach to dust particles, giving them a net negative charge. The charged particles migrate under the influence of an electric field ($E_c = 3$ to $6 \, kV/cm$) toward the positively charged collection plates. > 3. **Removal Stage:** Accumulated dust is dislodged by **rapping** (mechanical vibration) and falls into hoppers for disposal. **The Deutsch-Anderson Equation:** $$\boxed{\eta = 1 - e^{-A\omega/Q}}$$ Where: - $\eta$ = fractional collection efficiency - $A$ = total collection electrode area (m²) - $\omega$ = particle migration velocity (m/s) - $Q$ = gas volumetric flow rate (m³/s) **Factors Affecting ESP Efficiency:** | Factor | Effect on Efficiency | Engineering Implication | |---|---|---| | Gas flow rate ($Q$) | Lower Q → higher efficiency | Limit velocity to 1.0–1.5 m/s | | Specific Collection Area (SCA = A/Q) | Higher SCA → higher efficiency | Increase plate area or use 2 fields in series | | Particle migration velocity ($\omega$) | Higher $\omega$ → higher efficiency | Improved by resistivity tuning (10⁶–10¹⁰ Ω·cm) | | Particle resistivity | Too low (re-entrainment) or too high (back corona) | Add conditioning agents (SO₃, NH₃) | | Gas temperature | Affects viscosity and resistivity | Operate ESP above acid dew point | | Voltage applied | Higher → higher field → higher efficiency | Maximize secondary voltage; avoid sparkover | | Particle size | Larger particles have higher $\omega$ | Pre-conditioning with cyclones is optional | --- ### **Question B (Option 2):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) Explain the working of a fabric filter with a neat sketch. Discuss the role of filter cake in collection efficiency. [7 Marks]** **(b) A fabric filter handles 50,000 m³/h of gas. The filter bags are 6 m long and 0.15 m diameter. The pressure drop is 1.0 kPa. The bag resistance coefficient is 1.5 × 10⁷ m⁻¹ and the dust cake specific resistance is 5 × 10⁹ m/kg. The weight of dust on the filter is 0.4 kg/m². Calculate the total pressure drop. [7 Marks]** --- #### **Solution (a):** **Working of a Fabric Filter:** A fabric filter (baghouse) is a dry collection device that uses **fibrous filter media** (woven or felted) to capture particulates from a gas stream. **Stages of Operation:** 1. **Filtration:** Dust-laden gas enters the bag from below (or above) and flows **outward (or inward)** through the fabric. Particles are captured by: - **Inertial Impaction** — for particles > 1 μm - **Interception** — for 0.5–1 μm - **Diffusion** (Brownian) — for < 0.5 μm - **Electrostatic attraction** — for charged particles - **Gravitational settling** — minor contributor 2. **Cake Build-up:** Particles accumulate on the fabric surface, forming a porous dust cake. 3. **Cleaning Cycle:** When pressure drop reaches a setpoint (typically 100–200 mm WC), the filtration is interrupted and a **cleaning mechanism** activates: - **Reverse Air:** clean air flows backward through the bag. - **Shake-Deflate:** mechanical shaking + reverse air. - **Pulse-Jet:** short bursts of high-pressure compressed air. **Role of Filter Cake:** > [!IMPORTANT] > The dust cake itself becomes the **primary filter medium** during steady operation. It provides: > - **Higher collection efficiency** — the cake creates a tortuous path for particles. > - **Self-sealing action** — small cracks in the cake are quickly filled. > - **Higher pressure drop** — proportional to cake weight ($W$). > - **Depth filtration** — particles are captured throughout the cake thickness, not just at the surface. **Pressure Drop Model:** $$\Delta P = \Delta P_0 + K_c \cdot W$$ Where: - $\Delta P_0$ = clean fabric pressure drop - $K_c$ = specific cake resistance (m/kg) - $W$ = dust areal density (kg/m²) **Diagram (ASCII Schematic):** ``` [ Dirty Gas In ] ↓ ┌─────────┐ │ ⊕⊕⊕⊕⊕⊕ │ ← Filter Cake (collected dust) │ ──/─/── │ ← Fabric Media │ Bag │ │ │ └────┬────┘ ↓ [ Hopper ] [ Clean Gas Out → ] ``` --- #### **Solution (b):** **Given:** - $Q = 50{,}000 \, m^3/h$ - $L = 6 \, m$ (bag length) - $d = 0.15 \, m$ (bag diameter) - $\Delta P$ measured = 1.0 kPa (we are asked to verify) - Bag resistance coefficient = $1.5 \times 10^7 \, m^{-1}$ - $K_c = 5 \times 10^9 \, m/kg$ - $W = 0.4 \, kg/m^2$ **Step 1: Calculate the clean fabric pressure drop** The clean fabric pressure drop is given by the Darcy-type equation: $$\Delta P_0 = K_f \cdot \mu \cdot v_f$$ Assuming standard air viscosity $\mu = 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \, kg/(m \cdot s)$ and using the resistance coefficient in the form: $$\Delta P_0 = K_f \cdot v = 1.5 \times 10^7 \cdot v_f$$ For typical face velocity $v_f = 0.02 \, m/s$ (1.2 m/min): $$\Delta P_0 = 1.5 \times 10^7 \times 0.02 = 3 \times 10^5 \, Pa = 0.3 \, kPa$$ **Step 2: Calculate the cake contribution** $$\Delta P_{cake} = K_c \cdot W = 5 \times 10^9 \times 0.4 = 2 \times 10^9 \, Pa$$ > [!WARNING] > **This value is extremely high — it indicates the assumed face velocity is too low. The actual face velocity must be determined from the given data.** **Step 3: Use measured total ΔP to back-calculate face velocity** The total pressure drop equation: $$\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_0 + \Delta P_{cake}$$ Using $\Delta P_0 = K_f \cdot v_f$ and $\Delta P_{cake} = K_c \cdot v_f \cdot W$: $$\Delta P_{total} = v_f (K_f + K_c \cdot W)$$ $$\Delta P_{total} = v_f \left(1.5 \times 10^7 + 5 \times 10^9 \times 0.4\right)$$ $$\Delta P_{total} = v_f \left(1.5 \times 10^7 + 2 \times 10^9\right)$$ $$\Delta P_{total} = v_f \times 2.015 \times 10^9$$ **Step 4: Solve for $v_f$ given total ΔP = 1.0 kPa** $$v_f = \frac{1000}{2.015 \times 10^9} = 4.96 \times 10^{-7} \, m/s$$ **Step 5: Verification** - $\Delta P_0 = 1.5 \times 10^7 \times 4.96 \times 10^{-7} = 7.44 \, Pa$ - $\Delta P_{cake} = 2 \times 10^9 \times 4.96 \times 10^{-7} = 992.5 \, Pa$ - $\Delta P_{total} = 7.44 + 992.5 = 999.9 \, Pa \approx 1.0 \, kPa$ ✓ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The cake contributes **> 99%** of the total pressure drop. This confirms the principle that the **filter cake dominates filtration resistance** in a baghouse — which is why cake removal (filter cleaning) is critical to maintaining low operating pressure. --- ## 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION PITFALLS > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark Deductions in Module 3 Questions — DO NOT MAKE THESE MISTAKES:** > 1. **Forgetting $C_c$ for $d_p < 1 \, \mu m$** — Lose 1 mark. KTU examiners ALWAYS check this in gravity separator problems. > 2. **Mixing up "cut diameter" with "minimum particle diameter captured"** — These are NOT the same. Cut diameter = 50% collection; minimum = ~100% collection (typically 1.5× to 2× cut diameter). > 3. **Stating Deutsch-Anderson equation without specifying assumptions** — Must mention: (1) perfect mixing, (2) uniform $\omega$, (3) no re-entrainment. Lose 1 mark if missing. > 4. **Confusing primary and secondary collection in fabric filters** — Primary is on the clean filter (diffusion-dominated for submicron); secondary is via the cake (sieve + depth). > 5. **Wrong units in Stokes' law problem** — Always convert $\mu m$ to $m$ before computing; this costs 1 mark typically. > 6. **Forgetting that ESP efficiency depends on $(A/Q)$, not $A$ alone** — Doubling area is NOT the same as doubling efficiency; the relationship is exponential. > 7. **In scrubber design, ignoring the slip velocity correction** — The relative velocity between gas and droplet is critical for impaction. --- ## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP — RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final 5-Minute Revision — Memorize These Key Points:** - ✅ **Gas-Particle Interaction** is governed by drag, gravity, inertia, diffusion, and electrical forces. The relevant dimension is **aerodynamic diameter**. - ✅ **Stokes' Law:** $V_t = \frac{g d_p^2 (\rho_p - \rho_g) C_c}{18 \mu}$ — apply only when $Re_p < 1$. - ✅ **Slip Correction Factor** $C_c$ is **essential** for $d_p < 1 \, \mu m$ (Cunningham regime). - ✅ **Gravity Separators** are cheap but inefficient for particles < 50 μm; used as pre-cleaners. - ✅ **Cyclone Design Equation:** $d_{pc} = \sqrt{\frac{9 \mu W}{2 \pi N_t V_s (\rho_p - \rho_g)}}$ — Lapple's model. - ✅ **Cyclone Efficiency:** $\eta = \frac{1}{1 + (d_{pc}/d_p)^2}$ — useful for design grade efficiency curves. - ✅ **Fabric Filter Collection** uses diffusion, interception, impaction, and electrostatic attraction. Cake dominates resistance. - ✅ **Filter Pressure Drop:** $\Delta P = \Delta P_0 + K_c \cdot W$ — cake contribution is the dominant term in operation. - ✅ **ESP Deutsch-Anderson Equation:** $\eta = 1 - e^{-A\omega/Q}$ — exponential, not linear with area. - ✅ **Migration velocity** depends on charge, field, and gas viscosity: $\omega = \frac{q_p E_c}{3 \pi \mu d_p}$. - ✅ **Scrubbers** use droplet-particle **inertial impaction**; Calvert's model gives $d_{50}$ in terms of droplet diameter and slip velocity. - ✅ **Particle resistivity** is the key operating parameter for ESPs — target range $10^6$ to $10^{10} \, \Omega \cdot cm$. - ✅ **Selection Hierarchy:** For PM2.5 control, ESPs and Baghouses are preferred. For coarse dust, cyclones. For hot gases (e.g., $T > 250°C$), use ESPs (fabric filters limited). - ✅ **Corona onset** voltage: $V_0 = 31 \times 10^4 \cdot \rho_g \cdot d_w \cdot \ln(d_w/r_w)$ — depends on wire radius $r_w$ and wire-to-plate distance $d_w$. - ✅ **Cyclone Pressure Drop:** $\Delta P = \xi \cdot \frac{\rho_g V_s^2}{2}$ with $\xi = 8$ for standard cyclones, 6.4 for high-efficiency.

topics.title: Operational Considerations - Factors Affecting Selection of Control Equipment
Working Principle, Design, and Performance Equations of Absorption, Adsorption, Condensation, Incineration, Bio Scrubbers, Venturi Scrubber - Biofilters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBT726 - AIR POLLUTION CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Working Principle, Design, and Performance Equations of Absorption, Adsorption, Condensation, Incineration, Bio Scrubbers, Venturi Scrubber - Biofilters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITIONS & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Air Pollution Control Equipment — The Big Picture

Air pollution control (APC) devices are broadly classified into two categories based on the phase of the pollutant:
1. **Particulate Control Devices** (cyclones, ESPs, baghouses, scrubbers) — covered earlier in the syllabus.
2. **Gaseous Control Devices** — the focus of this module.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme (OEBT726), Module 4 deals exclusively with **gaseous pollutant removal techniques** governed by mass transfer, equilibrium thermodynamics, and biological/biochemical conversion principles.

The seven techniques under study belong to four fundamental removal mechanisms:

| Mechanism | Equipment | Driving Force |
|-----------|-----------|---------------|
| Mass transfer (gas → liquid) | **Absorber** | Concentration gradient |
| Mass transfer (gas → solid surface) | **Adsorber** | Surface affinity |
| Phase change (gas → liquid) | **Condenser** | Temperature reduction |
| Chemical destruction | **Incinerator** | High temperature oxidation |
| Mass transfer + biological oxidation | **Bio-scrubber** | Henry's law + microbial metabolism |
| Mass transfer (gas → liquid, high velocity) | **Venturi Scrubber** | Momentum transfer |
| Biological oxidation (single-stage) | **Biofilter** | Microbial degradation |

---

## 1.2 Absorption

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** Absorption is a unit operation in which one or more soluble components of a gas mixture are dissolved in a liquid (the absorbent or solvent). It is governed by **Henry's Law** and the **Two-Film Theory** of mass transfer.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine pouring tea through a sugar cube. The water (gas) absorbs the sugar (pollutant) — except here, a *liquid solvent* selectively captures a *gaseous pollutant* from a flue gas stream. The "sweetness" (concentration) in the liquid keeps rising until equilibrium.

- **Standard Metric:** Henry's constant **H** = **0.61 mol/(L·atm)** at 25 °C for CO₂ in water.
- **Common Equipment:** Packed tower, tray tower, venturi scrubber, spray tower.
- **Typical Applications:** SO₂ in limestone slurry, HCl in water, NH₃ in sulfuric acid, VOC in organic solvents.

---

## 1.3 Adsorption

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** Adsorption is a surface phenomenon in which molecules of a gas (the adsorbate) accumulate on the surface of a solid (the adsorbent) due to van der Waals forces (physisorption) or chemical bonding (chemisorption).

**Conceptual Analogy:** A dry sponge placed on a spilled ink droplet soaks the ink onto its internal pores — but the sponge doesn't change chemically. Similarly, **activated carbon** has millions of micropores (≈ 500–1500 m²/g surface area) that physically trap gas molecules.

- **Standard Metric:** BET surface area of activated carbon = **500–1500 m²/g**.
- **Common Adsorbents:** Activated carbon, silica gel, activated alumina, zeolites, molecular sieves.
- **Typical Applications:** Mercury vapor, dioxins/furans, VOCs, odor control, NO₂.

---

## 1.4 Condensation

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** Condensation is the phase-change process by which a vapor is converted into liquid by reducing its temperature below its dew point or by increasing its pressure. Used to recover VOCs from gas streams.

**Conceptual Analogy:** A cold mirror held over a boiling kettle fogs up — water vapor condenses on the cold surface. Industrial condensers do the same: cool the gas below its dew point and the VOC "rains out" as liquid.

- **Standard Metric:** Condensation efficiency η = **80–95 %** for refrigerative systems.
- **Two Types:** Surface condensers (heat transferred through a wall) and Contact condensers (cooling fluid mixes directly with gas).

---

## 1.5 Incineration (Thermal Oxidation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** Incineration (thermal oxidation) is the high-temperature oxidation of combustible gaseous, liquid, or particulate pollutants in a combustion chamber, converting them to CO₂, H₂O, and mineral residues.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Burning a kitchen gas leak before it accumulates dangerously. Industrial incinerators deliberately ignite waste gases at high temperature to break them into harmless products.

- **Standard Metric:** Destruction Removal Efficiency (DRE) ≥ **99.99 %** for hazardous waste.
- **Three Cardinal Rules (the 3 T's):** Temperature, Time, Turbulence.

---

## 1.6 Bio-Scrubber (Bioscrubber)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** A bio-scrubber is a **two-stage** air pollution control device that combines a wet scrubber (absorber) with a biological regeneration unit. The pollutant is first absorbed into an aqueous phase, then oxidized by microorganisms in a separate biological reactor.

**Conceptual Analogy:** A *fish-tank air filter*: air bubbles rise through water where microbes "eat" the dissolved pollutants, then clean water is recirculated back. The absorption and biodegradation happen in **separate vessels**.

---

## 1.7 Venturi Scrubber

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** A venturi scrubber is a high-energy wet scrubbing device that uses the *throat's* convergent–divergent geometry to accelerate gas to high velocity, atomize scrubbing liquid into fine droplets, and transfer particulates/gases from gas to liquid via impaction and diffusion.

**Conceptual Analogy:** A water hose with a pinched nozzle sprays water atomized into a fine mist — dust particles collide with the droplets and are washed down. The venturi geometry creates exactly this high-velocity, fine-droplet collision zone.

- **Standard Metric:** Pressure drop ΔP = **500–2500 mm H₂O** (very high energy device).
- **Collection Efficiency:** > **99 %** for sub-micron particles.

---

## 1.8 Biofilter

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition:** A biofilter is a **single-stage** biological air-treatment device in which a contaminated air stream passes through a moist porous medium (compost, peat, wood chips) on which a biofilm of microorganisms grows and degrades the pollutants.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Garden compost in a sealed bin — when odorous air passes through the damp organic material, microbes living on the material eat the odor-causing compounds. The whole process happens in **one vessel**, unlike the bio-scrubber.

- **Standard Metric:** Empty Bed Residence Time (EBRT) = **15–60 seconds** for VOCs.
- **Removal Efficiency:** **80–95 %** for biodegradable VOCs.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook — "Bio-scrubber vs Biofilter":**
> **Bio-scrubber = Bio + Scrubber (two vessels; liquid phase moves)**
> **Biofilter = Bio + Filter (one vessel; air passes through damp solid)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

---

## 2.1 ABSORPTION — Detailed Theory

### 2.1.1 Governing Principles

Absorption is governed by **two simultaneous phenomena**:

1. **Gas–Liquid Equilibrium** (thermodynamic limit) — described by **Henry's Law**:
$$C_A = H \cdot P_A$$

where C_A = dissolved concentration (mol/m³), H = Henry's constant (mol/(m³·Pa)), P_A = partial pressure of A.

2. **Mass Transfer Kinetics** (rate) — described by the **Two-Film Theory** of Whitman (1923):
$$N_A = K_G \cdot (P_A - P_A^*) = K_L \cdot (C_A^* - C_A)$$

where N_A = molar flux, K_G = overall gas-phase mass transfer coefficient, K_L = overall liquid-phase coefficient, superscript * denotes equilibrium.

### 2.1.2 The Operating Line and Material Balance

For a counter-current packed absorber with gas flow G (kg/h dry air-free) and liquid flow L (kg/h solvent-free):

$$G \cdot \frac{dY}{dZ} = K_y \cdot a \cdot (Y - Y^*)$$

Integrating across the tower height Z:

$$Z = \int_{Y_2}^{Y_1} \frac{G \cdot dY}{K_y \cdot a \cdot (Y - Y^*)}$$

### 2.1.3 Height of Transfer Unit (HTU) and Number of Transfer Units (NTU)

$$Z = H_{OG} \times N_{OG}$$

where:

$$H_{OG} = \frac{G}{K_y \cdot a}, \quad N_{OG} = \int_{Y_2}^{Y_1} \frac{dY}{Y - Y^*}$$

### 2.1.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Absorption

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $C_A = H \cdot P_A$ | Henry's Law | mol/m³ |
| $N_A = K_G \cdot (P_A - P_A^*)$ | Two-film gas-phase flux | mol/(m²·s) |
| $N_A = K_L \cdot (C_A^* - C_A)$ | Two-film liquid-phase flux | mol/(m²·s) |
| $\frac{1}{K_G} = \frac{1}{k_G} + \frac{m}{k_L}$ | Overall resistance | — |
| $Z = H_{OG} \cdot N_{OG}$ | Packed tower height | m |
| $H_{OG} = \frac{G}{K_y \cdot a}$ | HTU based on gas phase | m |
| $\frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{Y_1 - m X_2} = \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}$ | Kremser equation (gas) | — |
| $A = \frac{L}{m \cdot G}$ | Absorption factor | — |

---

## 2.2 ADSORPTION — Detailed Theory

### 2.2.1 Adsorption Isotherms

The equilibrium loading of adsorbate on adsorbent at constant temperature is given by an **isotherm**:

**Langmuir Isotherm** (monolayer, homogeneous sites):

$$q = \frac{q_m \cdot b \cdot C}{1 + b \cdot C}$$

where q = mass adsorbed (kg/kg), q_m = maximum capacity, b = affinity constant, C = equilibrium concentration.

**Freundlich Isotherm** (heterogeneous, empirical):

$$q = K_F \cdot C^{1/n}$$

### 2.2.2 Fixed-Bed Adsorber — Breakthrough Curve

A fixed bed shows three zones as it operates:
1. **Saturated Zone** (in equilibrium with feed) — at the inlet.
2. **Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ)** — where adsorption is occurring.
3. **Fresh Zone** — where no adsorption has yet occurred.

The breakthrough curve plots exit concentration C_out vs. time. Two key design parameters:

- **Breakthrough time** t_b: when C_out reaches permissible limit.
- **Saturation time** t_s: when C_out ≈ C_in.

### 2.2.3 Length of Unused Bed (LUB)

$$LUB = L \cdot \left(1 - \frac{t_b}{t_s}\right) = L \cdot \frac{t_s - t_b}{t_s}$$

### 2.2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Adsorption

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $q = \frac{q_m b C}{1 + b C}$ | Langmuir isotherm | kg/kg |
| $q = K_F \cdot C^{1/n}$ | Freundlich isotherm | (kg/kg)(m³/kg)^(1/n) |
| $\frac{W}{Q} = \frac{q_e (C_0 - C_e)}{C_0}$ | Bed capacity equation | kg/kg |
| $LUB = L \cdot \left(1 - \frac{t_b}{t_s}\right)$ | Length of unused bed | m |
| $t_b = \frac{\rho_b \cdot q \cdot L}{C_0 \cdot u}$ | Breakthrough time | s |
| $\theta = \frac{\rho_b \cdot q}{\rho_g \cdot u}$ | Contact time parameter | s |

---

## 2.3 CONDENSATION — Detailed Theory

### 2.3.1 Mechanisms

- **Surface condensation:** vapor condenses on a cooled surface (filmwise or dropwise).
- **Homogeneous condensation:** requires supersaturation.

### 2.3.2 Heat Transfer Basis

The condenser is designed as a **shell-and-tube heat exchanger**. The duty is:

$$Q = \dot{m}_g \cdot (h_1 - h_2) = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LM}$$

$$\Delta T_{LM} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$

### 2.3.3 Condensation Efficiency

$$\eta_c = \frac{y_{in} - y_{out}}{y_{in} - y_{eq}}$$

### 2.3.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Condensation

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LM}$ | Heat transfer in condenser | W |
| $\dot{m}_{cond} = \frac{Q}{h_{fg}}$ | Mass of vapor condensed | kg/s |
| $\eta_c = \frac{y_{in} - y_{out}}{y_{in} - y_{eq}}$ | Condensation efficiency | — |
| $P_{sat} = \exp\left(A - \frac{B}{T + C}\right)$ | Antoine equation | Pa |

---

## 2.4 INCINERATION — Detailed Theory

### 2.4.1 The 3-T Rule

| T | Typical Value |
|---|---------------|
| Temperature | **760–1200 °C** |
| Residence Time | **0.5–2.0 s** |
| Turbulence | Reynolds > 10 000 |

### 2.4.2 Combustion Stoichiometry

For a hydrocarbon C_xH_y:

$$C_xH_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) O_2 \rightarrow x \cdot CO_2 + \frac{y}{2} \cdot H_2O$$

### 2.4.3 Destruction Removal Efficiency (DRE)

$$DRE = \frac{W_{in} - W_{out}}{W_{in}} \times 100 \, \%$$

For RCRA compliance: DRE ≥ **99.99 %**.

### 2.4.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Incineration

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $DRE = \frac{W_{in} - W_{out}}{W_{in}} \times 100$ | Destruction removal efficiency | % |
| $\tau = \frac{V}{\dot{V}_g}$ | Residence time | s |
| $\eta_{th} = \frac{\dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot LHV}{\dot{Q}_{input}}$ | Thermal efficiency | — |
| $\phi = \frac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoich}}$ | Equivalence ratio (inverse) | — |

---

## 2.5 BIO-SCRUBBER — Detailed Theory

### 2.5.1 Two-Stage Configuration

**Stage 1:** Absorption column (scrubber) — gas pollutant dissolves into water.
**Stage 2:** Activated sludge bioreactor — microorganisms oxidize dissolved pollutant.

**Mass balance** across absorber:

$$G \cdot (Y_{in} - Y_{out}) = L \cdot (X_{out} - X_{in})$$

**Microbial growth** follows Monod kinetics:

$$\mu = \frac{\mu_{max} \cdot S}{K_s + S}$$

### 2.5.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Bio-Scrubber

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $C_L^* = \frac{P_G}{H}$ | Equilibrium liquid concentration | mol/m³ |
| $\mu = \frac{\mu_{max} \cdot S}{K_s + S}$ | Monod growth rate | 1/h |
| $r_s = -\frac{\mu_{max}}{Y_{x/s}} \cdot \frac{S \cdot X}{K_s + S}$ | Substrate removal rate | kg/(m³·h) |

---

## 2.6 VENTURI SCRUBBER — Detailed Theory

### 2.6.1 Contact-Power Theory (Semrau)

A venturi scrubber's collection efficiency depends on the **contact power** (power input per unit gas volume):

$$\eta = 1 - \exp\left(-\alpha \cdot P_t^{\beta}\right)$$

where P_t = contact power (W/m³) and α, β are empirical constants.

### 2.6.2 Pressure Drop (Holland's Equation)

$$\Delta P = \frac{\rho_g \cdot v_{th}^2}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{Q_L}{Q_G}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{L}{D}\right)$$

### 2.6.3 Cut Diameter (Calvert's Equation)

For particles, the cut diameter (50 % collection):

$$d_{pc} = \left(\frac{9 \cdot \mu_g \cdot D}{\rho_p \cdot v_{th} \cdot C}\right)^{1/2}$$

### 2.6.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Venturi Scrubber

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $\eta = 1 - \exp(-\alpha P_t^{\beta})$ | Contact-power efficiency | — |
| $P_t = \frac{Q_G \cdot \Delta P}{V}$ | Contact power density | W/m³ |
| $\Delta P = \frac{\rho_g v_{th}^2}{2}$ | Throat pressure drop | Pa |
| $d_{pc} = \sqrt{\frac{9 \mu_g D}{\rho_p v_{th} C}}$ | Cut diameter | m |
| $\text{SCR} = \frac{\dot{V}_L}{Q_G}$ | Liquid-to-gas ratio | L/m³ |

---

## 2.7 BIOFILTER — Detailed Theory

### 2.7.1 Operating Principle

A biofilter is a **packed-bed biofilm reactor**. Air flows through a moist organic support; pollutants diffuse into the biofilm where they are biodegraded.

### 2.7.2 Empty Bed Residence Time (EBRT)

$$EBRT = \frac{V_{bed}}{Q_g}$$

### 2.7.3 Removal Efficiency (Plug-Flow Model)

$$\frac{C_{out}}{C_{in}} = \exp(-k \cdot EBRT)$$

where k is the overall first-order biodegradation rate constant.

### 2.7.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Biofilter

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $EBRT = V_{bed}/Q_g$ | Empty bed residence time | s |
| $C_{out}/C_{in} = \exp(-k \cdot EBRT)$ | Plug-flow outlet | — |
| $\delta = \sqrt{D_s/k}$ | Biofilm penetration depth | m |
| $\frac{dC}{dt} = -k_1 C$ | First-order degradation | g/(m³·s) |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exam: Compare EBRT of biofilter (15–60 s) with bio-scrubber (1–10 s in scrubber stage + 5–20 h sludge retention). Biofilter is simpler but limited to biodegradable, low-concentration pollutants.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Kremser Equation for Counter-Current Absorber

The **Kremser equation** is the most important analytical result for absorption design and is *regularly* asked in KTU 14-mark problems. We derive it step by step.

### Statement
For a counter-current gas absorption tower with N theoretical stages, given inlet/outlet gas compositions Y₁, Y₂ and inlet liquid composition X₂, find the number of stages required for a chosen absorbent rate L (or vice-versa).

### Setup

Let:
- G = molar gas flow (kmol/s, solute-free basis)
- L = molar liquid flow (kmol/s, solvent-free basis)
- Y = mole ratio of solute in gas (kmol solute / kmol gas)
- X = mole ratio of solute in liquid (kmol solute / kmol liquid)
- m = slope of equilibrium line Y* = m X

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Operating Line Equation** from material balance on the top section:

$$G \cdot (Y - Y_1) = L \cdot (X - X_1)$$

Solving for Y:

$$Y = Y_1 + \frac{L}{G}(X - X_1)$$

**Step 2 — Mass Balance Between Stages** (j-th and (j+1)-th):

$$G \cdot Y_j + L \cdot X_{j+1} = G \cdot Y_{j+1} + L \cdot X_j$$

Rearranging:

$$Y_{j+1} - Y_j = \frac{L}{G}(X_{j+1} - X_j)$$

**Step 3 — Equilibrium at Each Stage:**

$$Y_j^* = m \cdot X_j \quad \Rightarrow \quad X_j = Y_j^*/m$$

**Step 4 — Define Absorption Factor:**

$$A = \frac{L}{m \cdot G}$$

This is dimensionless and represents the ratio of liquid capacity to gas capacity.

**Step 5 — Recursive Solution.** Substituting the operating line back into the stage balance and applying recursively from stage 1 to N:

$$Y_2 = \frac{Y_1 + A \cdot Y_2^*}{A + 1}$$

After N stages, one obtains the **Kremser equation**:

$$\boxed{\frac{Y_{N+1} - Y_1^*}{Y_N - Y_1^*} = \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}}$$

Equivalently (commonly written form for absorption):

$$\boxed{\frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{Y_1 - m X_2} = \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}}$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A counter-current absorber must remove **95 %** of acetone from air using water. Inlet gas: 2 mol % acetone in air; L = 1.5 G; equilibrium slope m = 1.6. Find the number of theoretical stages.

**Solution:**

Given:
- Y₁ = 2/98 = **0.0204** kmol acetone / kmol air
- Y₂ = 0.05 × Y₁ = 0.00102
- X₂ = 0 (pure water inlet)
- A = L/(mG) = 1.5/1.6 = **0.9375**

Substituting into Kremser:

$$\frac{0.0204 - 0.00102}{0.0204 - 0} = \frac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1} = 0.950$$

Let r = A^{N+1}. Then:

$$0.950 = \frac{r - 0.9375}{r - 1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.950(r - 1) = r - 0.9375$$

$$0.950 r - 0.950 = r - 0.9375 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.9375 - 0.950 = r - 0.950 r$$

$$-0.0125 = 0.050 r \quad \Rightarrow \quad r = -0.25$$

**Note:** Negative value indicates A < 1 (L too small). For A < 1, infinite stages are theoretically required. The student would conclude: increase L until A > 1, or use a stripping factor approach. In practice, this is a **limiting case** — the design is governed by the minimum L/G ratio:

$$\left(\frac{L}{G}\right)_{min} = m \cdot \frac{Y_1 - Y_2}{X_1^* - X_2}$$

For practical operation, choose **L/G = 1.4 × (L/G)_min**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Length of Unused Bed (LUB) in Adsorption

### Statement
For a fixed-bed adsorber, the breakthrough curve defines the portion of bed still unused when breakthrough occurs. Show that LUB can be computed from the breakthrough and saturation times.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the wave-front.** As gas flows, an adsorption front moves down the bed at velocity v_f. Behind the front, the bed is in equilibrium with feed (saturation loading q*).

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric time t_s** — when entire bed would reach saturation:

$$t_s = \frac{\rho_b \cdot q^* \cdot L}{C_0 \cdot u_g}$$

where ρ_b = bed density, u_g = superficial gas velocity, L = bed depth.

**Step 3 — Real breakthrough time t_b** is less than t_s because the mass transfer zone (MTZ) has finite width. Mass balance on solute in bed:

$$\rho_b \cdot q^* \cdot L \cdot A = C_0 \cdot u_g \cdot A \cdot t_s \quad \text{(stoichiometric)}$$

**Step 4 — Saturation time t_s corresponds to** when the MTZ just leaves the bed. At breakthrough, only a fraction (1 − f) of MTZ has left, where f = breakthrough fraction.

**Step 5 — Geometry of MTZ.** If we assume constant-pattern behavior, the MTZ length Z_m is constant. When the leading edge of MTZ reaches the bed exit (saturation), t = t_s. When the trailing edge reaches the exit, t = t_b + Z_m/v_f.

The fractional use of bed at breakthrough:

$$f_b = \frac{t_b}{t_s}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{LUB = L \cdot (1 - f_b) = L \cdot \left(1 - \frac{t_b}{t_s}\right)}$$

### KTU Numerical Example

A 2.0 m activated-carbon bed breaks through a toluene stream at t_b = 6 h and saturates at t_s = 9 h. Find:
(i) LUB, (ii) corrected bed depth for 95 % effective use.

**Solution:**
(i) LUB = 2.0 × (1 − 6/9) = **0.667 m**
(ii) Corrected depth = 2.0 / 0.95 = 2.105 m, using LUB = 0.105 m effectively. The extra 0.105 m replaces the unused portion.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: DRE and Stoichiometric Air for Incineration

### Problem
A waste gas stream contains 200 kg/h of methane (CH₄) at 25 °C. Air is supplied at 25 % excess. Compute the minimum combustion temperature (adiabatic flame temperature) at constant pressure.

**Stoichiometric combustion:**

$$CH_4 + 2 O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + 2 H_2O$$

**Molar mass CH₄ = 16 kg/kmol**, so 200/16 = **12.5 kmol/h** of CH₄.
**O₂ stoichiometric = 25 kmol/h.**
**Air (21 % O₂, 79 % N₂)** required stoichiometrically = 25/0.21 = **119.05 kmol/h**.
With 25 % excess: 1.25 × 119.05 = **148.81 kmol/h** of air.

**Energy balance** (adiabatic, no work):

$$\sum_i n_i \int_{T_{in}}^{T_f} C_{p,i} \, dT = -\Delta H_{rxn}$$

Using constant-pressure enthalpies (298 K reference):

| Component | n (kmol/h) | ΔH_f (kJ/kmol) | C_p (kJ/kmol·K) |
|-----------|-----------|----------------|------------------|
| CH₄ (in) | 12.5 | −74 850 | 35.7 |
| O₂ (in) | 31.25 | 0 | 29.4 |
| N₂ (in) | 117.55 | 0 | 29.0 |
| CO₂ (out) | 12.5 | −393 520 | 37.1 |
| H₂O (out) | 25 | −241 830 | 33.6 |
| N₂ (out) | 117.55 | 0 | 29.0 |

Heat released by reaction:

$$Q = [12.5(393520) + 25(241830)] - [12.5(74850) + 0] = 10\,961\,750 \, \text{kJ/h}$$

Energy to heat products:

$$Q = [12.5(37.1) + 25(33.6) + 117.55(29.0)](T_f - 298)$$

$$Q = [463.75 + 840 + 3408.95](T_f - 298) = 4712.7 (T_f - 298)$$

Equating:

$$T_f - 298 = \frac{10\,961\,750}{4712.7} = 2325.8 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_f \approx 2624 \, \text{K}$$

Adiabatic flame temperature ≈ **2351 °C** — well above the 1200 °C limit, so combustion is feasible.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Venturi Scrubber Collection Efficiency

### Contact-Power Theory (Semrau, 1958)
